1:
"And Saul was consenting unto his death. And at that time there was a great persecution against the church which was at Jerusalem; and they were all scattered abroad throughout the regions of Judaea and Samaria, except the apostles." The witnesses note Saul's consent to Stephen's death and the great scattering of the church. Gill presses the force of "consenting": it "signifies not a bare consent only, but a consent with pleasure and delight; he was well pleased with it, it rejoiced his very heart"; and notes this is mentioned to magnify the grace shown in Saul's later conversion. The witnesses note the great persecution scattering the church through Judea and Samaria, all but the apostles fleeing. The witnesses note Saul's consent and the scattering.

The witnesses note Saul's pleased consent to Stephen's death and the great persecution scattering the church.

So the witnesses make the verse Saul's consent and the scattering of the church. The lesson the expositors draw is the violence of the persecution, the depth of Saul's guilt (magnifying his later grace), and the providence that scattered the church. The witnesses note Saul's consent: "Saul was consenting unto his death" — Gill presses that the word means not a bare consent, but a consent with pleasure and delight; Saul was well pleased with Stephen's murder, his heart rejoicing in it; he kept the clothes of the witnesses who stoned him (Acts 7:58). The expositors observe why this is recorded: to magnify the grace that would later convert this very Saul — the persecutor who delighted in the martyr's blood made the apostle of the Gentiles; the depth of his sin the foil to the riches of grace. The witnesses note the persecution: "a great persecution against the church which was at Jerusalem" — a fierce and widespread assault, breaking out at Stephen's death; the church, till now favored by the people, now violently persecuted. The expositors press the scattering: "they were all scattered abroad throughout the regions of Judaea and Samaria, except the apostles" — the believers fled the city, dispersed through the surrounding regions; only the apostles remained, whether by courage, by duty to the mother church, or by providence keeping them at the post of danger. The witnesses observe the providence in the scattering: what the enemies meant to destroy the church served to spread it; the scattered believers carried the gospel wherever they went (v.4); the persecution the very means of the gospel's expansion beyond Jerusalem, fulfilling Christ's word that the witness should reach Judea and Samaria (Acts 1:8). The expositors draw the lesson on God overruling persecution: the assault designed to crush the church became the occasion of its enlargement; God turning the wrath of men to his praise (Psalm 76:10), the scattering the seed of wider harvest. The witnesses note the apostles remaining: their staying, when all others fled, a mark of their courage and their charge to hold the ground at Jerusalem; the shepherds at their post while the flock dispersed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the depth of Saul's guilt and the grace that met it, the violence of the persecution, and the providence that scattered the church to spread the gospel. The verse thus records Saul's consent and the scattering — the great persecution dispersing the church — teaching the depth of Saul's sin, the providence overruling persecution, and the scattering that spread the gospel.
2:
"And devout men carried Stephen to his burial, and made great lamentation over him." The witnesses note the pious burial and mourning of Stephen. Gill identifies the "devout men" as members of the Christian church, "eminent for their religion and piety, who had courage enough, amidst this persecution, to show a respect to the dead body of this holy martyr"; they prepared the body for burial and mourned him; and notes burying the dead was reckoned among acts of kindness and piety. The witnesses note godly men, braving the persecution, giving Stephen an honorable burial and great mourning — the church honoring its first martyr. The witnesses note the burial of Stephen. The witnesses note the lamentation.

The witnesses note devout men bravely burying Stephen and mourning him — the church honoring its first martyr.

So the witnesses make the verse the burial and mourning of Stephen. The lesson the expositors draw is the courage and piety of those who honored the martyr, and the fitness of mourning the godly dead. The witnesses note the actors: "devout men carried Stephen to his burial" — Gill identifies them as Christian men, eminent in piety, who, amid the raging persecution, had courage to honor the body of the martyr; braving the danger to give Stephen an honorable burial. The expositors observe the courage this required: to show respect to one stoned as a blasphemer, in the height of the persecution, was to risk being marked as his fellow; yet these devout men, undeterred, prepared and buried the body; their piety overcoming their fear. The witnesses note the care of the burial: they carried Stephen to his burial, preparing the body decently (Gill notes, washing and wrapping it), and gave him an honorable interment; burying the dead being reckoned among the acts of kindness and piety. The expositors press the mourning: "made great lamentation over him" — they mourned the martyr greatly, with deep and open grief; the loss of so godly a man, the first to die for Christ, calling forth their tears. The witnesses observe the fitness of mourning the godly dead: though Stephen was with Christ, gloriously departed (Acts 7:59-60), the church mourned his loss — not as those without hope, but with the natural and proper grief of love; the saints may and should mourn the death of the godly, even while rejoicing in their glory. The expositors draw the lesson on honoring the faithful dead: the church honored its first martyr with burial and mourning; a pattern of respect for the godly departed, and of the love that grieves their loss while resting in their gain. The witnesses note the contrast with the persecutors: while Saul made havoc and the enemies raged, the devout men honored the martyr; the love of the saints against the fury of the foes. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the courage and piety of those who honored Stephen, the fitness of mourning the godly dead, and the love that braves danger to honor the faithful. The verse thus records the burial and mourning of Stephen — devout men burying him with great lamentation — teaching the courage and piety of honoring the martyr and the fitness of mourning the godly dead.
3:
"As for Saul, he made havock of the church, entering into every house, and haling men and women committed them to prison." The witnesses note the fury of Saul's persecution. Barnes presses the violence: the word "made havock" (elymaineto) "is commonly applied to wild beasts, to lions, wolves... and denotes the devastations which they commit; Saul raged against the church like a wild beast" — a strong expression of his zeal and fury; "entering into every house" to search out Christians, "haling" (dragging) them, committing them to prison. The witnesses note Saul ravaging the church like a wild beast, dragging men and women to prison — the height of his persecuting fury. The witnesses note Saul's fury. The witnesses note the havoc of the church.

The witnesses note Saul ravaging the church like a wild beast — dragging men and women to prison.

So the witnesses make the verse the fury of Saul's persecution. The lesson the expositors draw is the violence and zeal of Saul against the church, and the depth of the sin that grace would conquer. The witnesses note the savagery: "he made havock of the church" — Barnes presses that the word is used of wild beasts, of lions and wolves ravaging; Saul raged against the church like a wild beast, devastating it; a strong expression of his fury and zeal. The expositors observe the thoroughness of his persecution: "entering into every house" — Saul searched house to house for those suspected of being Christians, sparing no place; his zeal exhaustive, hunting down the believers wherever they hid. The witnesses note the cruelty: "haling men and women committed them to prison" — dragging them by force (Gill notes the violence), without regard to sex, committing both men and women to prison; the brutality of treating women as well as men with such violence, the mark of an unrestrained fury. The expositors draw the lesson on the depth of Saul's sin: the future apostle was, at this time, a savage persecutor, ravaging the church like a beast, imprisoning the innocent; "I persecuted this way unto the death, binding and delivering into prisons both men and women" (Acts 22:4); the chief of sinners (1 Timothy 1:15) in his persecuting rage. The witnesses observe the foil this provides to grace: the very man who made havoc of the church would become its greatest builder; the wild beast tamed into a lamb, the persecutor into the apostle; the depth of his sin magnifying the riches of the grace that converted him. The expositors note the providence in recording this: Luke sets down Saul's fury, that the wonder of his conversion (Acts 9) might be the more felt; the havoc he made the dark background of the light that would shine on him. The witnesses note the suffering of the early church: the believers ravaged, imprisoned, scattered; the church baptized in persecution from its youth; the cost of confessing Christ in that hour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the fury of Saul's persecution, the depth of the sin that grace would conquer, and the suffering of the early church under his rage. The verse thus records the fury of Saul's persecution — making havoc, haling men and women to prison — teaching the violence of his zeal, the depth of his sin, and the foil it provides to the grace that would convert him.
4:
"Therefore they that were scattered abroad went every where preaching the word." The witnesses note the gospel spread by the scattered believers. Matthew Henry draws the riddle unriddled: "Out of the eater comes forth meat, and out of the strong sweetness. The persecution that was designed to extirpate the church was by the overruling providence of God made an occasion of the enlargement of it"; they thought "by scattering those who were kindled with that fire to have put it out, but instead of this they did but help to spread it." The witnesses note the scattered disciples going everywhere preaching the word — the persecution spreading the gospel. The witnesses note the gospel spread by the scattered. The witnesses note the preaching everywhere.

The witnesses note the scattered believers preaching everywhere — the persecution spreading the very gospel it meant to crush.

So the witnesses make the verse the gospel spread by the scattered believers. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that turned the persecution into the means of the gospel's spread. The witnesses note the action: "they that were scattered abroad went every where preaching the word" — the believers, dispersed by the persecution, did not hide in fear or fall silent, but went everywhere preaching the gospel; the scattering becoming the sowing of the word. The expositors press Matthew Henry's beautiful figure (from Samson's riddle, Judges 14:14): "out of the eater comes forth meat, and out of the strong sweetness" — the persecution that was designed to destroy the church was overruled by God to enlarge it; the enemies thought, by scattering those kindled with the fire of the gospel, to put it out, but instead helped to spread it; the very means of intended destruction the means of growth. The witnesses observe the courage and zeal of the scattered: they did not go to hide themselves, but to preach; their faith undeterred by persecution, their tongues unsilenced by danger; the fire of the gospel burning in them wherever they were driven. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence overruling persecution: God turns the wrath of his enemies to his praise (Psalm 76:10); the scattering, meant for harm, carried the gospel beyond Jerusalem to Judea, Samaria, and beyond — the persecution the very engine of the mission, fulfilling Christ's word (Acts 1:8). The witnesses note who these preachers were: not the apostles (who remained at Jerusalem, v.1), but the ordinary believers, the scattered disciples; the gospel spread not by the apostles only, but by the whole church, every believer a witness, preaching the word wherever he went. The expositors observe the pattern this sets: the spread of the gospel the work of the whole church, not the ministers only; every scattered believer a missionary, carrying the word; the church advancing through the witness of all its members. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providence that turned persecution to the gospel's spread, the courage of the scattered preachers, and the calling of every believer to witness wherever he is. The verse thus records the gospel spread by the scattered — going everywhere preaching the word — teaching the providence that made persecution the means of the gospel's spread and the witness of the whole church.
5:
"Then Philip went down to the city of Samaria, and preached Christ unto them." The witnesses note Philip's mission to Samaria. Lightfoot identifies this Philip as "the second amongst the seven deacons" (Acts 6:5), not the apostle; and discusses which city of Samaria. The witnesses note Philip, one of the seven, carrying the gospel to Samaria — a bold step, for the Jews had no dealings with the Samaritans (John 4:9); preaching Christ to a people the Jews despised, the gospel beginning to cross the old barriers. The witnesses note Philip preaching Christ in Samaria. The witnesses note the mission to the Samaritans.

The witnesses note Philip preaching Christ in Samaria — the gospel crossing the old barrier to the despised Samaritans.

So the witnesses make the verse Philip's mission to Samaria. The lesson the expositors draw is the advance of the gospel across the old barrier to the Samaritans, and the substance of Philip's preaching — Christ. The witnesses note the preacher: "Philip" — Lightfoot identifies him as the second of the seven deacons (Acts 6:5), not the apostle; one of the scattered believers (v.4) now carrying the gospel to Samaria; the deacon become an evangelist. The expositors observe the significance of the field: "the city of Samaria" — Philip went to the Samaritans, a people the Jews despised and shunned ("the Jews have no dealings with the Samaritans," John 4:9); the gospel now crossing the old, deep barrier of Jewish-Samaritan enmity, reaching a people long estranged. The witnesses press the boldness and the breadth of this step: that a Jewish believer should preach to the Samaritans was a bold crossing of the ancient division; the gospel beginning to break down the middle wall (Ephesians 2:14), advancing from Jerusalem to Samaria as Christ had foretold (Acts 1:8); the Samaritan mission a step toward the Gentiles. The expositors draw the lesson on the substance of Philip's preaching: "preached Christ unto them" — Philip preached not himself, not a system, but Christ; the person and work of the Saviour the center of his message; "we preach Christ crucified" (1 Corinthians 1:23); the gospel the proclamation of Christ. The witnesses observe the fitness of preaching Christ to the Samaritans: the Samaritans looked for the Messiah (John 4:25, "I know that Messias cometh"); and Philip preached Christ to them, the very One they awaited; the gospel meeting their hope. The expositors note the providence in the Samaritan mission: Christ himself had sown there (John 4), and now Philip reaps; the field prepared, the harvest gathered; the gospel advancing where the Lord had gone before. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the gospel crossing the old barrier to the despised Samaritans, the boldness of the mission, and Christ as the substance of all true preaching. The verse thus records Philip's mission to Samaria — preaching Christ to the Samaritans — teaching the advance of the gospel across the old barrier and the preaching of Christ as its substance.
6:
"And the people with one accord gave heed unto those things which Philip spake, hearing and seeing the miracles which he did." The witnesses note the Samaritans' eager attention and the miracles that confirmed the word. Clarke notes Philip "had fixed their attention, not only with the gravity and importance of the matter of his preaching, but also by the miracles which he did." The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the imperfect "gave heed" — they "kept on giving heed, holding the mind on the things said by Philip, spell-bound." The witnesses note the people unitedly attending to Philip's preaching, drawn by both his words and the miracles he wrought. The witnesses note the eager attention. The witnesses note the confirming miracles.

The witnesses note the Samaritans eagerly heeding Philip — drawn by his preaching and the confirming miracles.

So the witnesses make the verse the Samaritans' eager attention. The lesson the expositors draw is the power of the word joined to the miracles, drawing the people to give heed. The witnesses note the unity and eagerness: "the people with one accord gave heed unto those things which Philip spake" — the Samaritans, unitedly and eagerly, attended to Philip's preaching; Robertson notes the imperfect, that they kept on giving heed, holding their minds on his words, spell-bound. The expositors observe the double draw, which Clarke marks: Philip fixed their attention both by the gravity and importance of his preaching and by the miracles he wrought; the word and the works together compelling their attention. The witnesses press the role of the miracles: "hearing and seeing the miracles which he did" — the people heard the word and saw the miracles; the miracles confirming the message, attesting that Philip's preaching was of God; the works opening the way for the word. The expositors draw the lesson on the confirmation of the gospel by signs: in that age, the miracles of the evangelists confirmed their message, attesting the gospel as divine; the Samaritans, seeing the miracles, gave heed to the words; the signs the credentials of the messenger. The witnesses observe the union of hearing and seeing: the people both heard the preaching and saw the miracles; the two together — the word to the ear, the works to the eye — drawing their whole attention; the gospel commended to them by both. The expositors note the eager, united response: the Samaritans gave heed "with one accord" — unitedly, eagerly, with attentive minds; a readiness to hear that prepared them for faith (the next verses); the good soil receiving the seed. The witnesses draw the lesson on the right hearing of the word: to "give heed" is to attend with the mind, to receive the word seriously; the Samaritans' eager attention the disposition through which faith would come; the attentive hearing the way to believing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the power of the word joined to the works, the eager attention of the Samaritans, and the right hearing that prepares for faith. The verse thus records the Samaritans' eager attention — giving heed to Philip's word and miracles — teaching the power of the word joined to the confirming miracles, drawing the people to give heed.
7:
"For unclean spirits, crying with loud voice, came out of many that were possessed with them: and many taken with palsies, and that were lame, were healed." The witnesses note the casting out of demons and the healings. Clarke presses the reality of demonic possession: "hence it is evident that these unclean spirits were not a species of diseases, as they are here distinguished from the paralytic and the lame; there is nothing more certain than that the New Testament writers mean real diabolic possessions by the terms unclean spirits, devils, etc.; it is absolute trifling to deny it." The witnesses note the demons cast out and the diseased healed — the miracles attesting Philip's word. The witnesses note the casting out of demons. The witnesses note the healings.

The witnesses note demons cast out and the sick healed — the miracles distinguishing real possession from disease.

So the witnesses make the verse the casting out of demons and the healings. The lesson the expositors draw is the reality of demonic possession, distinct from disease, and the power of Christ over both through Philip. The witnesses note the two kinds of miracle: "unclean spirits... came out of many that were possessed" — the casting out of demons; "and many taken with palsies, and that were lame, were healed" — the healing of diseases; the demoniacs delivered and the sick cured. The expositors press Clarke's argument for the reality of demonic possession: the unclean spirits are here distinguished from the palsied and the lame — the possessed set apart from the merely diseased; so the "unclean spirits" are not a name for diseases, but real demons; the New Testament writers mean real diabolic possessions, and to deny it (reducing possession to mere illness) is, Clarke says, absolute trifling. The witnesses observe the force of the distinction: had possession been mere disease, the text would not distinguish the possessed from the palsied and lame; the separate mention proves the demons real, distinct from the diseases also healed; the casting out of demons a victory over the powers of darkness, not the curing of an illness. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ's power over the demonic: the unclean spirits, crying with loud voice (in rage and reluctance), came out at Philip's word in Christ's name; the powers of darkness subject to the name of Jesus; the gospel delivering men from the bondage of Satan as well as from disease. The witnesses note the loud crying of the demons: they came out "crying with loud voice" — in rage, reluctance, and defeat; the unclean spirits forced out against their will, their loud cries the token of their unwilling submission to the power of Christ. The expositors observe the comprehensiveness of the deliverance: both spiritual bondage (possession) and bodily affliction (palsy, lameness) were relieved; the gospel bringing wholeness to body and the deliverance of those enslaved by Satan; Christ's power over all the works of the devil (1 John 3:8). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the reality of demonic possession (against its reduction to disease), the power of Christ over the demons through Philip, and the comprehensive deliverance the gospel brings. The verse thus records the casting out of demons and the healings — unclean spirits cast out, the sick healed — teaching the reality of possession distinct from disease and the power of Christ over both.
8:
"And there was great joy in that city." The witnesses note the joy that the gospel brought to Samaria. Barnes traces its sources: the joy arose from the healing of the sick and afflicted, from the conversion of individuals to Christ, and from the mutual joy of families that their friends were converted; "the tendency of religion is to produce joy... the tendency of a revival of religion is thus to produce great joy." Clarke notes, "no wonder, when they heard such glorious truths, and were the subjects of such beneficent miracles." The witnesses note the great joy in the city — the fruit of the gospel and its miracles. The witnesses note the great joy. The witnesses note the joy of the gospel.

The witnesses note the great joy in Samaria — the fruit of the gospel, its conversions, and its mercies.

So the witnesses make the verse the joy that the gospel brought. The lesson the expositors draw is that the gospel produces joy — in the healing of the afflicted, the conversion of souls, and the gladness of families. The witnesses note the result: "there was great joy in that city" — the gospel, preached and confirmed by miracles, filled the city with joy; Samaria, long under the sway of Simon's sorcery, now rejoicing in the gospel. The expositors trace the sources of this joy, which Barnes names: the joy of the healed (so many sick and afflicted restored), the joy of the converted (souls turned to Christ), and the mutual joy of families and friends (rejoicing that their loved ones were saved); the gospel bringing gladness on every side. The witnesses press the tendency of religion to produce joy: Barnes notes that the tendency of true religion, and especially of a revival, is to produce great joy; the gospel no message of gloom, but of glad tidings; its fruit joy in the saved and in those who love them. The expositors observe Clarke's note: no wonder there was great joy, when the people heard such glorious truths and were the subjects of such beneficent miracles; the joy the natural fruit of the gospel's blessings, both spiritual and bodily. The witnesses draw the lesson on the joy of salvation: the gospel brings joy — the joy of forgiveness, of new life, of healing, of fellowship; the kingdom of God "righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost" (Romans 14:17); the great joy in Samaria a token of the gospel's gladdening power. The expositors note the contrast with Samaria's former state: under Simon's sorcery, the people had been bewitched, deceived, in bondage; now, under the gospel, they had joy; the gospel bringing the joy that sorcery and deception could never give. The witnesses observe the corporate joy: "great joy in that city" — not the joy of a few only, but of the city; a city-wide gladness, the gospel transforming the whole community; the joy of a people turning to Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the joy the gospel brings, its sources in healing, conversion, and family gladness, and the joy of salvation that no deception can give. The verse thus records the great joy in Samaria — the fruit of the gospel and its mercies — teaching that the gospel produces joy in the healed, the converted, and those who love them.
9:
"But there was a certain man, called Simon, which beforetime in the same city used sorcery, and bewitched the people of Samaria, giving out that himself was some great one:" The witnesses note Simon the sorcerer and his self-exaltation. Gill notes Simon was a Samaritan, of the village of Gitton, a wizard. Lightfoot discusses how Simon had gained such a hold on the Samaritans. The witnesses note Simon, who by sorcery had long bewitched the people, giving out that he was some great one — a deceiver exalting himself, the great rival to the gospel in Samaria. The witnesses note Simon the sorcerer. The witnesses note his self-exaltation.

The witnesses note Simon the sorcerer — who by his magic had long bewitched Samaria and exalted himself.

So the witnesses make the verse the introduction of Simon the sorcerer. The lesson the expositors draw is the deceiving power of Simon's sorcery and self-exaltation, the great rival to the gospel in Samaria. The witnesses note the man: "a certain man, called Simon, which beforetime in the same city used sorcery" — Simon, a Samaritan (Gill notes, of the village of Gitton), a practitioner of sorcery, magic, and deceptive arts; long established in the city before Philip came. The expositors observe his hold on the people: "and bewitched the people of Samaria" — by his sorceries he had bewitched, amazed, and deceived the Samaritans; holding them in a kind of fascination, his magic arts winning their wonder and submission; the people long under his sway. The witnesses press his self-exaltation: "giving out that himself was some great one" — Simon claimed to be some great person, exalting himself, drawing the people's admiration to himself; the deceiver seeking his own glory, setting himself up as an object of wonder. The expositors draw the lesson on the deceiver's pride and self-seeking: Simon, like all false teachers and impostors, sought his own exaltation; "giving out that himself was some great one" — the mark of the deceiver, who points men to himself, not to God; the opposite of Philip, who preached Christ (v.5), not himself. The witnesses note the contrast Simon provides to the true gospel: Simon by sorcery deceived and bewitched, exalting himself; Philip by the gospel and true miracles enlightened and saved, preaching Christ; the false against the true, the deceiver against the evangelist. The expositors observe the danger Simon represented: the great rival to the gospel in Samaria, holding the people in deception; his hold to be broken by the power of the gospel (v.12); the false miracle-worker confronted by the true. The witnesses note the providence in the contest: God sent the gospel to a city long deceived by sorcery, that the true power might overcome the false; the light of Christ dispelling the darkness of Simon's deception. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the deceiving power of false teachers, their self-exaltation, and the gospel that overcomes deception with truth. The verse thus records the introduction of Simon the sorcerer — bewitching Samaria, exalting himself — teaching the deceiving power and self-exaltation of the false, the great rival to the gospel.
10:
"To whom they all gave heed, from the least to the greatest, saying, This man is the great power of God." The witnesses note the people's universal regard for Simon and their extravagant claim. Clarke notes a textual reading: in many manuscripts the word "called" is added, so that the passage reads, "This person is that power of God which is called the Great" — the people ascribing to Simon a divine power. He notes "they certainly did not believe him to be God, but thought him endued with a great supernatural power." The witnesses note all the people, high and low, regarding Simon and calling him the great power of God — the depth of their deception. The witnesses note the universal regard. The witnesses note the extravagant claim.

The witnesses note all Samaria regarding Simon as "the great power of God" — the depth of their deception.

So the witnesses make the verse the people's universal regard for Simon. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth and breadth of the deception, all the people ascribing divine power to the sorcerer. The witnesses note the universality: "to whom they all gave heed, from the least to the greatest" — all the people, high and low, rich and poor, regarded Simon; the deception complete, embracing the whole city, none exempt from his influence; the same word "gave heed" (v.6) that they would later give to Philip. The expositors observe the extravagant claim: "This man is the great power of God" — the people ascribed to Simon a divine power, calling him the great power of God; Clarke notes a textual reading adding "called," so that it reads "that power of God which is called the Great" — the people regarding Simon as the embodiment of a divine power. The witnesses press Clarke's clarification: the people did not believe Simon to be God himself, but thought him endued with a great supernatural power, an agent or embodiment of divine power; their regard idolatrous, exalting the sorcerer to a divine status. The expositors draw the lesson on the depth of the deception: the whole city, deceived by Simon's sorcery, ascribed to him divine power; the deception so complete that they made the deceiver an object of near-worship; the darkness of a people without the gospel, deceived by false signs. The witnesses observe the danger of false signs: Simon's sorceries, mistaken for divine power, deceived the people; a warning that signs and wonders may deceive, and that men may ascribe to deceivers what belongs to God alone; "false Christs and false prophets shall... shew great signs and wonders" (Matthew 24:24). The expositors note the contrast about to come: the people called Simon "the great power of God" — but when Philip preached Christ and wrought true miracles, they would turn from Simon to believe the gospel (v.12); the false power eclipsed by the true; the deceiver's hold broken by the gospel. The witnesses observe the providence in the contest: the people, long deceived, would be undeceived by the gospel; the true power of God (in Christ) overcoming the false power they had ascribed to Simon; the light exposing the darkness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the depth of the deception, the danger of false signs, and the gospel that exposes the false and reveals the true power of God. The verse thus records the people's regard for Simon — all calling him the great power of God — teaching the depth of the deception and the false power to be overcome by the true gospel.
11:
"And to him they had regard, because that of long time he had bewitched them with sorceries." The witnesses note the long-established hold of Simon's deception. Ellicott notes "the long time during which the evil fascination had been exercised"; the people held under Simon's spell by his sorceries over a long period. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the same word "had regard" as the "gave heed" before. The witnesses note the people's long-standing regard for Simon, bewitched by his sorceries over a long time — the depth and duration of the deception that the gospel would overcome. The witnesses note the long deception. The witnesses note Simon's established hold.

The witnesses note Simon's long-established hold — the people bewitched by his sorceries over a long time.

So the witnesses make the verse the long-established hold of Simon's deception. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth and duration of the deception, which made the triumph of the gospel the more remarkable. The witnesses note the people's regard: "to him they had regard" — the same word as the "gave heed" of the foregoing verses; the people held in regard, attention, and submission to Simon. The expositors observe the duration: "because that of long time he had bewitched them with sorceries" — Simon's hold was not recent, but long-established; Ellicott notes the long time during which the evil fascination had been exercised; the people had been under his spell for a considerable period, deeply and habitually deceived. The witnesses press the depth this implies: a deception of long standing, rooted in habit and time; the people not lightly or briefly deceived, but long held under Simon's sorceries; the fascination deep and settled, hard to break. The expositors draw the lesson on the difficulty of overcoming established error: a long-held deception is not easily broken; the people, habituated to Simon's sorceries, were deeply bound; the triumph of the gospel over so settled a deception the more remarkable, a proof of its power. The witnesses observe the contrast with what follows: despite the long and deep hold of Simon, when Philip preached Christ, the people turned from Simon to believe (v.12); the gospel breaking a deception that years had established; the power of the word overcoming the bondage of long sorcery. The expositors note the providence in the long deception: God permitted the people to be long deceived, that the power of the gospel in delivering them might be the more manifest; the deeper the darkness, the brighter the light that dispelled it. The witnesses observe the warning in the long deception: men may be long and deeply held by error, by false teachers, by deceptions of long standing; the bondage of habit and time strong; yet the gospel can break even the most settled deception. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the depth and duration of Simon's hold, the difficulty of overcoming established error, and the power of the gospel that breaks even long deception. The verse thus records the long-established hold of Simon — the people bewitched of long time — teaching the depth and duration of the deception that the gospel would overcome.
12:
"But when they believed Philip preaching the things concerning the kingdom of God, and the name of Jesus Christ, they were baptized, both men and women." The witnesses note the Samaritans' conversion and baptism. Gill marks the power that turned them: though long attached to Simon, "when Philip came and preached Christ unto them, such was the power that attended his ministry, and such the efficacy of divine grace, that they not only gave heed but believed." Clarke notes Philip's word "came with greater power than that of Simon." The witnesses note the people, turning from Simon, believing Philip's preaching of the kingdom and the name of Jesus, and being baptized — men and women. The witnesses note the conversion and baptism. The witnesses note the gospel's triumph.

The witnesses note the Samaritans believing and baptized — the gospel triumphing over Simon's long deception.

So the witnesses make the verse the Samaritans' conversion and baptism. The lesson the expositors draw is the triumph of the gospel over the long deception, the people believing and being baptized. The witnesses note the turning: "when they believed Philip preaching the things concerning the kingdom of God, and the name of Jesus Christ" — the people, long held by Simon, now believed Philip's preaching; the gospel of the kingdom and of Christ winning their faith. The expositors observe the power that effected this, which Gill marks: though the people had long been attached to Simon and held in his deception, when Philip preached Christ, such was the power attending his ministry and the efficacy of divine grace, that they not only gave heed but believed; the gospel breaking the long-established deception; Clarke notes Philip's word came with greater power than Simon's. The witnesses press the substance of the faith: they believed "the things concerning the kingdom of God, and the name of Jesus Christ" — the gospel of the kingdom and of Christ; not a vague impression, but faith in the message of the kingdom and the name of Jesus; the true object of saving faith. The expositors draw the lesson on the power of the gospel over deception: the gospel, attended by the Spirit's grace, overcame the long deception of sorcery; the true power of God (in the word and Spirit) eclipsing the false power they had ascribed to Simon; the people delivered from deception to truth, from Simon to Christ. The witnesses note the baptism: "they were baptized, both men and women" — the believers, upon their faith, were baptized; the outward sign following the inward faith; men and women alike received into the church by baptism; the proper order — faith, then baptism. The expositors observe the inclusion of both sexes: "both men and women" — the gospel embracing all, men and women alike believing and baptized; the breadth of the gospel's reach, none excluded. The witnesses observe the contrast with their former state: the people who had ascribed divine power to Simon now believed in Christ and were baptized; the deceived undeceived, the bewitched delivered, the city turned from sorcery to the gospel; the triumph of the truth. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the gospel's triumph over deception, the faith in the kingdom and the name of Jesus, and the baptism that follows faith. The verse thus records the Samaritans' conversion and baptism — believing Philip, baptized men and women — teaching the triumph of the gospel over the long deception and the baptism that follows faith.
13:
"Then Simon himself believed also: and when he was baptized, he continued with Philip, and wondered, beholding the miracles and signs which were done." The witnesses note Simon's profession of faith and its nature. Gill judges it was faith only "with an historical and temporary faith... or at least he pretended to believe"; not saving faith. Lightfoot agrees Simon "believed that Jesus of Nazareth was the true Messiah, and so was made capable of baptism," but no more. The witnesses note Simon professing faith and being baptized, yet continuing to wonder at the miracles — a faith that was historical and temporary, not saving; the hypocrite within the church. The witnesses note Simon's professed faith. The witnesses note the temporary faith.

The witnesses note Simon professing faith and baptized — yet a historical, temporary faith, not saving.

So the witnesses make the verse Simon's profession of faith and its nature. The lesson the expositors draw is the difference between professed and saving faith, displayed in Simon, the hypocrite within the church. The witnesses note the profession: "Simon himself believed also: and when he was baptized" — Simon, like the others, professed faith and was baptized; outwardly joining the church, receiving the sign of baptism. The expositors weigh the nature of his faith, and judge it not saving: Gill holds it was a historical and temporary faith — Simon believed that Jesus was the Messiah (a notional assent), or at least pretended to believe; Lightfoot agrees Simon believed Jesus to be the Messiah and so was capable of baptism, but no more; not the saving faith of the heart, but a notional, temporary, or feigned belief. The witnesses press the marks against his faith being saving: the subsequent narrative (v.18-23) shows Simon "in the gall of bitterness and bond of iniquity," his heart not right with God; Peter declaring he had no part in the matter; so his faith, whatever it seemed, was not the faith that saves; a profession without a renewed heart. The expositors draw the lesson on professed versus saving faith: a man may believe with the head, assent to the truth, profess faith, and be baptized, yet not have the saving faith of the heart; Simon the great example of the hypocrite within the church, baptized yet unconverted; "a form of godliness, but denying the power" (2 Timothy 3:5). The witnesses observe the token in his "wondering": "he continued with Philip, and wondered, beholding the miracles" — Simon, the former sorcerer, was amazed at the true miracles, which surpassed his own arts; his wonder the wonder of the magician at a greater power, not the faith of the converted heart; his interest in the miracles, not in Christ. The expositors note the danger Simon represents: the baptized hypocrite, within the church, professing faith, yet unregenerate; a warning that baptism and profession are no proof of saving faith; the visible church containing the false among the true. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the difference between professed and saving faith, the example of Simon the baptized hypocrite, and the warning that profession and baptism do not save. The verse thus records Simon's profession and baptism — a historical, temporary faith — teaching the difference between professed and saving faith and the hypocrite within the church.
14:
"Now when the apostles which were at Jerusalem heard that Samaria had received the word of God, they sent unto them Peter and John:" The witnesses note the apostles' sending of Peter and John to Samaria. Lightfoot notes (with Epiphanius) that "Philip, being but a deacon, had not the power of imposition of hands, so as by that to confer the gift of the Holy Ghost; it was the apostles' peculiar province and prerogative, by laying on of their hands, to communicate the Holy Ghost," that is, his extraordinary gifts. The witnesses note the Jerusalem apostles, hearing of Samaria's conversion, sending Peter and John to confer the Spirit — the apostolic confirmation of the Samaritan mission. The witnesses note the sending of the apostles. The witnesses note the apostolic confirmation.

The witnesses note the apostles sending Peter and John — to confer the Spirit and confirm the Samaritan mission.

So the witnesses make the verse the sending of Peter and John to Samaria. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostolic confirmation of the Samaritan mission and the apostles' peculiar power to confer the Spirit's gifts. The witnesses note the occasion: "when the apostles which were at Jerusalem heard that Samaria had received the word of God" — the apostles, remaining at Jerusalem (v.1), heard of the conversion of Samaria; the news of the gospel's advance reaching the mother church. The expositors observe the sending: "they sent unto them Peter and John" — the apostles sent two of their number, Peter and John, to Samaria; the Jerusalem church confirming and embracing the new converts; the apostolic sanction given to the Samaritan mission. The witnesses press Lightfoot's point (with Epiphanius) on the reason: Philip, being a deacon and evangelist, had power to preach and baptize, but not to confer the gift of the Holy Ghost (the extraordinary gifts) by laying on of hands; this was the apostles' peculiar province and prerogative; so Peter and John were sent to do what Philip could not — to confer the Spirit's gifts. The expositors draw the lesson on the apostolic confirmation: the sending of the apostles to Samaria confirmed and authenticated the new mission, joining the Samaritan believers to the one church under the apostles; the gospel's advance to a new people sanctioned by the apostolic visit; the unity of the church maintained, the Samaritans not a separate body but joined to the whole. The witnesses observe the providence in this confirmation: the Samaritan mission, crossing the old barrier, was authenticated by the apostles, that there might be no division between Jewish and Samaritan believers; the apostolic visit binding the new converts to the one body; the unity of the church across the old enmity. The expositors note the significance of Peter and John in particular: the two leading apostles sent, the weight of the apostolic authority given to the Samaritan mission; the confirmation by the chief apostles. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the apostolic confirmation of the Samaritan mission, the apostles' peculiar power to confer the Spirit's gifts, and the unity of the church maintained across the old barrier. The verse thus records the sending of Peter and John — to confer the Spirit and confirm the mission — teaching the apostolic confirmation of the Samaritan mission and the apostles' prerogative to confer the Spirit's gifts.
15:
"Who, when they were come down, prayed for them, that they might receive the Holy Ghost:" The witnesses note the apostles' prayer for the Spirit and which gift is meant. Barnes presses the careful distinction: "the main question here is, what was meant by the Holy Ghost?" — and answers it was not the gift "by which the soul is converted or renewed, for they had this when they believed" (v.6, 12); but the extraordinary gifts. He notes the apostles "prayed for them... they did not even pretend to the power of doing it without the aid of God." The witnesses note Peter and John praying that the Samaritans might receive the Holy Ghost — the extraordinary gifts, distinct from the saving grace they already had. The witnesses note the prayer for the Spirit. The witnesses note the gift distinguished.

The witnesses note the apostles praying for the Spirit — the extraordinary gifts, distinct from the saving grace already received.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' prayer for the Spirit. The lesson the expositors draw is the careful distinction between the saving grace already received and the extraordinary gifts now sought, and the apostles' dependence on God in prayer. The witnesses note the prayer: "when they were come down, prayed for them, that they might receive the Holy Ghost" — Peter and John, on reaching Samaria, prayed that the believers might receive the Holy Ghost; seeking the gift from God by prayer. The expositors press Barnes's careful distinction about which gift is meant: not the saving, renewing grace of the Spirit, by which the soul is converted — for the Samaritans had this already, having believed (v.6, 12); but the extraordinary gifts of the Spirit (tongues, prophecy, miraculous powers), which were conferred in that age by the laying on of the apostles' hands; the gift here sought a distinct, extraordinary endowment, not the regenerating grace already possessed. The witnesses draw the lesson on the distinction: the Samaritans were already true believers, regenerated by the Spirit (their faith and baptism proving it); what they lacked, and what the apostles sought for them, was the extraordinary, visible gift of the Spirit — the miraculous endowments that marked the apostolic age; so this passage is not a pattern for a "second blessing" or a delay of the saving Spirit, but the conferring of the special gifts. The expositors observe the apostles' dependence on God: "prayed for them" — the apostles did not presume to confer the gift by their own power, but prayed to God for it; even they sought the gift from God, not pretending to bestow it of themselves; the gift God's to give, the apostles his instruments. The witnesses note the humility this shows: the apostles, with all their authority, prayed; the gift came from God in answer to prayer, not from the apostles' inherent power; the laying on of hands (v.17) the appointed means, but God the giver. The expositors draw the lesson on prayer as the means of receiving the Spirit's gifts: the apostles sought the gift by prayer; the Spirit's gifts are God's to bestow, sought by prayer; the dependence on God in all the work of the church. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the distinction between saving grace and the extraordinary gifts, the apostles' dependence on God in prayer, and the gift of the Spirit as God's to give. The verse thus records the apostles' prayer for the Spirit — that the Samaritans might receive the Holy Ghost — teaching the distinction between saving grace and the extraordinary gifts and the apostles' dependence on God in prayer.
16:
"(For as yet he was fallen upon none of them: only they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.)" The witnesses note that the Samaritans had been baptized but not yet received the extraordinary gift. Gill explains: they had received the Spirit "as a spirit of illumination and sanctification... they had been regenerated, enlightened, and sanctified by him... they were baptized believers, and no more; as yet none of them had gifts qualifying them for the ministry," nor could speak with tongues or work miracles; the Holy Ghost had "not yet descended on them for such purposes." The witnesses note the Samaritans truly converted and baptized, yet not yet endued with the extraordinary gifts. The witnesses note the gift not yet given. The witnesses note the baptized believers.

The witnesses note the Samaritans baptized and converted — yet not yet endued with the extraordinary gifts.

So the witnesses make the verse the distinction between the conversion already received and the gift not yet given. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Samaritans were truly converted and baptized, yet had not yet received the extraordinary, visible gift of the Spirit. The witnesses note the statement: "as yet he was fallen upon none of them: only they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus" — the extraordinary gift of the Spirit had not yet come on any of them; they had only been baptized as believers. The expositors press Gill's careful explanation: the Samaritans had indeed received the Spirit in his saving work — as a spirit of illumination, sanctification, conversion, and faith; they had been regenerated, enlightened, sanctified, brought to believe; they were true, baptized believers. What they had not yet received was the extraordinary gift — the gifts qualifying for the ministry, the speaking with tongues, the working of miracles; the Holy Ghost had not yet descended on them for these purposes. The witnesses draw the lesson, guarding against misreading: this verse does not mean the Samaritans were unconverted, or that the saving Spirit was withheld; they were already true believers, having the Spirit's saving grace; the gift not yet given was the extraordinary, visible endowment, distinct from the regenerating grace they possessed; the passage describes the conferring of special gifts, not the delay of salvation. The expositors observe the phrase "baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus": the Samaritans were baptized as Christians, into the name of Jesus; their baptism valid, their faith real; "only" baptized — that is, they had the baptism and the faith, but not yet the extraordinary gift. The witnesses note the providence in this arrangement: God ordered that the extraordinary gift should come not at Philip's baptizing, but at the apostles' laying on of hands — that the Samaritan mission might be confirmed by the apostles, the new converts joined to the one church under the apostolic authority; the delay serving the unity and confirmation of the church. The expositors draw the lesson on the distinction of the Spirit's works: the saving grace of the Spirit (regeneration, faith) is given to all believers; the extraordinary gifts were a special, temporary endowment of the apostolic age; the two distinct, the former universal, the latter peculiar to that time. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Samaritans as true converts already having the saving Spirit, the extraordinary gift not yet given, and the distinction between saving grace and the special gifts. The verse thus records the gift not yet given — baptized but not yet endued — teaching that the Samaritans were truly converted yet had not received the extraordinary gift, distinct from their saving grace.
17:
"Then laid they their hands on them, and they received the Holy Ghost." The witnesses note the conferring of the Spirit's gifts by the apostles' hands. Gill notes the apostles laid hands "not upon all of them, men and women, only on some they were directed unto by the Spirit of God; whom he had designed... to qualify for the work of the ministry," with both ministerial and extraordinary gifts; for the supply and confirmation of the new church. The witnesses note the apostles, by laying on of hands, conferring the Holy Ghost — the extraordinary gifts — on the Samaritan believers; the apostolic means of the gift. The witnesses note the laying on of hands. The witnesses note the gift conferred.

The witnesses note the apostles conferring the Spirit's gifts by laying on of hands — the apostolic means of the gift.

So the witnesses make the verse the conferring of the Spirit's gifts by the apostles' hands. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostolic means of conferring the extraordinary gift and its purpose for the new church. The witnesses note the act: "then laid they their hands on them, and they received the Holy Ghost" — the apostles laid their hands on the believers, and they received the Holy Ghost; the extraordinary gift conferred through the apostolic laying on of hands. The expositors observe Gill's note on the recipients: the apostles laid hands not on all, men and women, but on some — those directed to by the Spirit, whom God had designed to qualify for the ministry; the gift given to those appointed to serve the new church, with both ministerial and extraordinary gifts; for the supply and confirmation of the Samaritan church. The witnesses press the apostolic means: the gift came through the laying on of the apostles' hands — the peculiar prerogative of the apostles (v.14); the means by which the extraordinary gifts were conferred in that age; the apostles the appointed channel, God the giver (they having prayed, v.15). The expositors draw the lesson on the purpose of the gift: the extraordinary gifts were given for the establishment and confirmation of the new church — to qualify ministers, to attest the gospel, to build up the Samaritan believers; the gifts serving the church's foundation in that apostolic age. The witnesses observe the careful reading, against making this a universal pattern: this conferring of the extraordinary gift by the apostles' hands was peculiar to the apostolic age and to the apostles; not a pattern for an ordinary "confirmation" conferring the saving Spirit (the Samaritans already had saving grace); the extraordinary gifts and the apostolic means alike belonging to the foundation of the church. The expositors note the providence in the gift coming through the apostles: that the Samaritan mission might be confirmed and the new church joined to the apostolic body; the gift the seal of the church's unity and the confirmation of the gospel in Samaria. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the apostolic means of the extraordinary gift, its purpose for the new church, and the careful distinction from any ordinary pattern. The verse thus records the conferring of the Spirit's gifts — the apostles laying on hands, the believers receiving the Holy Ghost — teaching the apostolic means of the extraordinary gift and its purpose for the new church.
18:
"And when Simon saw that through laying on of the apostles' hands the Holy Ghost was given, he offered them money," The witnesses note Simon's covetous offer and what it reveals. Gill infers from Simon's seeing that the gift "was something visible and discernible, and so something external," falling under his notice. Barnes notes Simon "saw the extraordinary effects, the power of speaking in a miraculous manner," and, seeing the value of this power for his influence and gain, "offered them money"; the old sorcerer thinking, as men of his craft, to make money. The witnesses note Simon, seeing the visible gift conferred, offering the apostles money to buy the power — the sin that bears his name. The witnesses note Simon's offer. The witnesses note the visible gift.

The witnesses note Simon offering money for the gift — the covetous heart revealed, the sin of simony born.

So the witnesses make the verse Simon's covetous offer. The lesson the expositors draw is the revelation of Simon's unregenerate, covetous heart, and the origin of the sin of simony. The witnesses note what Simon saw: "when Simon saw that through laying on of the apostles' hands the Holy Ghost was given" — Gill infers the gift was visible and discernible, something external (the speaking with tongues, the miraculous effects), falling under Simon's notice; Barnes notes he saw the extraordinary effects, the power of speaking miraculously. The expositors observe Simon's response: "he offered them money" — Simon, seeing the value of this power, offered the apostles money to buy it; the old sorcerer, as men of his craft, thinking to make money and increase his influence by the power. The witnesses press what this reveals of Simon's heart: his offer betrayed his unregenerate state; he saw the gift not as a grace to be sought from God, but as a power to be bought and used for his own glory and gain; the covetous, ambitious heart of the sorcerer, unchanged by his profession and baptism; the hypocrite exposed by his offer. The expositors draw the lesson on the origin of simony: from Simon this sin takes its name — "simony," the buying and selling of spiritual things, offices, or gifts for money; Simon the first and the type of all who would traffic in holy things for gain; the sin of treating the gifts of God as merchandise. The witnesses observe the depth of Simon's error: he thought the gift of the Holy Ghost could be bought with money — a gross misunderstanding of the nature of God's gifts, which are not for sale; and (v.19) he sought not the gift for himself but the power to confer it on others, an even greater presumption. The expositors note the contrast with the apostles: the apostles had prayed for the gift (v.15), seeking it from God; Simon would buy it with money, treating it as a commodity; the difference between the gracious heart that seeks and the covetous heart that would purchase. The witnesses observe the exposure of the hypocrite: Simon's baptism and profession (v.13) had not changed his heart; his offer revealed the covetousness and ambition still ruling him; the false convert unmasked by his covetousness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the covetous heart revealed in Simon's offer, the origin of the sin of simony, and the warning against trafficking in holy things. The verse thus records Simon's covetous offer — offering money for the gift — teaching the revelation of his unregenerate heart and the origin of the sin of simony.
19:
"Saying, Give me also this power, that on whomsoever I lay hands, he may receive the Holy Ghost." The witnesses note the presumption of Simon's request. Gill notes Simon asked not that hands be laid on him to receive the gift, "but that he might have the power of conferring them on others." Lightfoot marks the depth of his error: "how infinitely mistaken is this wretch, if he think that the gifts of the Holy Ghost could be bought... and how much more mistaken still, if he think that the power of conferring these gifts could be thus attained"; even the apostles could not enable another to confer them. The witnesses note Simon seeking to buy the very power of conferring the Spirit — a double presumption. The witnesses note Simon's presumptuous request. The witnesses note the depth of his error.

The witnesses note Simon seeking to buy the power of conferring the Spirit — a double, presumptuous error.

So the witnesses make the verse the presumption of Simon's request. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of Simon's error and ambition, seeking to buy the very power of conferring the Spirit. The witnesses note the request: "Give me also this power, that on whomsoever I lay hands, he may receive the Holy Ghost" — Gill marks that Simon asked not merely to receive the gift himself, but to have the power of conferring it on others; not the gift, but the apostolic power, was his aim. The expositors press the double presumption Lightfoot exposes: Simon was infinitely mistaken in thinking the gifts of the Holy Ghost could be bought with money; and even more mistaken in thinking the power of conferring them could be so attained; for even the apostles could not enable another to confer the gifts — Paul could give Timothy the gifts by laying on hands, but could not make Timothy able to confer them on others; the power Simon sought was peculiar to the apostles, not transferable, not for sale. The witnesses draw the lesson on Simon's ambition: he sought not the grace of the Spirit for his own soul, but the power to confer the Spirit on others — that he might exalt himself, regain his influence, and be again "some great one" (v.9); the ambition of the deceiver, seeking power and glory, not grace. The expositors observe the depth of his error about God's gifts: Simon treated the gift and the power of the Spirit as commodities to be bought and used for self-advancement; a gross and impious misunderstanding, reducing the holy things of God to merchandise and tools of ambition; the very opposite of the humble, gracious heart. The witnesses note the contrast with true ministry: the apostles conferred the gift as God's instruments, by prayer and the laying on of hands; Simon would buy the power to wield it for himself; the difference between the servant of God and the ambitious self-seeker. The expositors note the providence in the exposure: Simon's request laid bare his heart, that Peter might rebuke him and the church be warned; the presumptuous offer the occasion of the solemn rebuke to follow (v.20-23). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the depth of Simon's error and ambition, the impossibility of buying or transferring God's gifts, and the contrast between gracious ministry and ambitious self-seeking. The verse thus records the presumption of Simon's request — to buy the power of conferring the Spirit — teaching the depth of his error and ambition and the impossibility of purchasing the holy gifts of God.
20:
"But Peter said unto him, Thy money perish with thee, because thou hast thought that the gift of God may be purchased with money." The witnesses note Peter's stern rebuke of Simon. Barnes explains the force: it is "an expression of the horror and indignation of Peter," not properly an imprecation; "let your money go to destruction; we abhor your impious offer"; yet also "an expression of his belief that Simon also would perish," hastening to ruin. He notes the rebuke of treating "the gift of God" as purchasable. The witnesses note Peter's vehement rejection of Simon's offer — abhorring the thought that God's gift could be bought, and declaring Simon's perilous state. The witnesses note Peter's rebuke. The witnesses note the gift not for sale.

The witnesses note Peter's stern rebuke — abhorring the thought that God's gift could be bought, and warning of Simon's ruin.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's stern rebuke of Simon. The lesson the expositors draw is the abhorrence of treating God's gift as purchasable and the warning of Simon's perilous state. The witnesses note the vehemence: "Thy money perish with thee" — Barnes explains this is an expression of Peter's horror and indignation, not properly an imprecation or curse; the sense, "let your money go to destruction; we abhor your impious offer; we would see any amount of money destroyed before we would sell the gift of the Holy Ghost"; the apostle's contempt for the money and abhorrence of the offer. The expositors observe the deeper meaning Barnes notes: there was also in the words an expression of Peter's belief that Simon himself would perish — that he was hastening to ruin; "thy money perish with thee" carrying the solemn warning of Simon's danger; the man as well as his money on the road to destruction. The witnesses press the ground of the rebuke: "because thou hast thought that the gift of God may be purchased with money" — Simon's sin was the impious thought that God's gift could be bought; the gift of God, freely given by grace, is not for sale; to think it purchasable is to profane it, to misunderstand utterly the nature of grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the freeness of God's gift: the gift of the Holy Ghost, and all the gifts of grace, are freely given, not bought; "the gift of God" — by definition a gift, not merchandise; to offer money for it is to insult the grace that gives it freely; "without money and without price" (Isaiah 55:1). The witnesses observe the sternness fitting the sin: Peter, abhorring the impiety, rebukes Simon sharply; the gravity of the sin (treating holy things as commodities, the covetous ambition) calling for severe reproof; the apostle not gentle with so profane an offer. The expositors note the warning of ruin: "perish with thee" — Peter warns that Simon, in his present state, was perishing; the man's danger declared, that he might be brought to repentance (v.22); the stern word a warning, not a final sentence. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the abhorrence of treating God's gift as purchasable, the freeness of grace, and the warning of ruin to the covetous and unregenerate. The verse thus records Peter's stern rebuke — thy money perish with thee — teaching the abhorrence of buying God's gift, the freeness of grace, and the warning of Simon's perilous state.
21:
"Thou hast neither part nor lot in this matter: for thy heart is not right in the sight of God." The witnesses note Peter's declaration of Simon's exclusion and its ground. Gill explains "this matter" as the business of the gift of the Holy Ghost, or the word of the Gospel and its blessings (forgiveness, righteousness, eternal life); Simon had no part or lot in it, the phrase answering a common Jewish expression for having "no part in the world to come." The witnesses note Peter declaring Simon has no share in the gospel and its blessings, because his heart is not right with God — the root of his exclusion. The witnesses note Simon's exclusion. The witnesses note the heart not right.

The witnesses note Peter's verdict: Simon has no part in the gospel, for his heart is not right with God.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's declaration of Simon's exclusion. The lesson the expositors draw is that Simon, despite his profession and baptism, had no share in the gospel, because his heart was not right with God. The witnesses note the exclusion: "Thou hast neither part nor lot in this matter" — Gill explains "this matter" as the gift of the Holy Ghost, and more broadly the word of the gospel and its blessings (forgiveness, justifying righteousness, eternal life); Simon had no part or lot in any of it; the phrase answering a Jewish expression for having "no part in the world to come." The expositors observe the force of the verdict: Simon, though baptized and professing faith (v.13), is declared to have no share in the gospel's blessings; his profession empty, his baptism availing nothing; the false convert excluded from the very thing he professed. The witnesses press the ground of the exclusion: "for thy heart is not right in the sight of God" — the root of Simon's exclusion was his heart; not right with God, not renewed, not sincere; whatever his outward profession, his heart was wrong, and so he had no part in the gospel. The expositors draw the lesson on the necessity of a right heart: the heart must be right with God; outward profession, baptism, and church-membership avail nothing without a renewed and sincere heart; "the LORD looketh on the heart" (1 Samuel 16:7); Simon's wrong heart the proof and cause of his exclusion. The witnesses observe the exposure of the hypocrite: Simon's offer (v.18-19) had revealed his heart — covetous, ambitious, unregenerate; and Peter, discerning it, declares his heart not right; the false convert unmasked, his inward state laid bare by his action. The expositors note the searching nature of God's judgment: "in the sight of God" — God sees the heart, however men are deceived by the outward profession; Simon might pass among men as a believer, but in the sight of God his heart was not right; the judgment of God penetrating to the heart. The witnesses draw the lesson on self-examination: every professor must examine whether his heart is right with God; for a wrong heart, however hidden, excludes from the gospel's blessings; the warning of Simon a call to sincerity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the necessity of a right heart, the emptiness of profession without it, and the searching judgment of God that sees the heart. The verse thus records Peter's verdict — no part in this matter, the heart not right — teaching that Simon was excluded from the gospel because his heart was not right with God, and the necessity of a renewed heart.
22:
"Repent therefore of this thy wickedness, and pray God, if perhaps the thought of thine heart may be forgiven thee." The witnesses note Peter's call to Simon to repent and pray. Barnes draws several remarks: "Simon was at this time an unconverted sinner; the command was given to him as such; he was required to do the thing — not to wait or seek merely, but actually to repent." The witnesses note Peter, even after the stern rebuke, calling Simon to repent of his wickedness and pray for forgiveness — the door of mercy held open even to so great a sinner, if he will repent. The witnesses note the call to repentance. The witnesses note the offer of mercy.

The witnesses note Peter calling Simon to repent and pray — the door of mercy held open even to the great sinner.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's call to Simon to repent and pray. The lesson the expositors draw is the offer of mercy even to the great sinner, on condition of repentance, and the duty of repentance commanded. The witnesses note the command: "Repent therefore of this thy wickedness" — Peter, even after the stern rebuke (v.20-21), calls Simon to repent; his sin was great wickedness (offering money for God's gift, with ambitious design), of which he must repent; the call to repentance held out even to so great an offender. The expositors press Barnes's remarks: Simon was at this time an unconverted sinner; the command to repent was given to him as such; he was required actually to repent, not merely to wait or seek; the duty of repentance laid plainly on him, to be done, not deferred. The witnesses note the second command: "and pray God, if perhaps the thought of thine heart may be forgiven thee" — Simon is to pray for forgiveness; repentance and prayer the way to pardon; the door of mercy held open, that even this great sin might be forgiven if he repent and pray. The expositors draw the lesson on the offer of mercy to the great sinner: Peter, having pronounced Simon's heart not right, yet calls him to repent and pray, holding out the hope of forgiveness; no sinner, however great, is beyond the offer of mercy if he will repent; the door of grace open even to Simon. The witnesses observe the "if perhaps": "if perhaps the thought of thine heart may be forgiven thee" — not that forgiveness was doubtful on God's part for the truly penitent, but the expression marks the greatness of the sin and the uncertainty of Simon's repentance; the "if perhaps" a solemn word, fitting the gravity of the offense and the doubt of the sinner's heart; yet the door not shut. The expositors note the balance Peter strikes: he neither flatters Simon (declaring his heart not right) nor despairs of him (calling him to repent and pray); the stern rebuke joined to the offer of mercy; conviction and invitation together. The witnesses draw the lesson on repentance and prayer as the way to forgiveness: the sinner is to repent of his sin and pray for pardon; the way of mercy open to the penitent; even the great sinner called to this way. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the offer of mercy even to the great sinner, the duty of repentance commanded, and repentance and prayer as the way to forgiveness. The verse thus records Peter's call to Simon — repent and pray, if perhaps forgiven — teaching the offer of mercy to the great sinner on condition of repentance and the way of forgiveness through repentance and prayer.
23:
"For I perceive that thou art in the gall of bitterness, and in the bond of iniquity." The witnesses note Peter's discernment of Simon's unregenerate state. Gill expounds the phrases: "in the gall of bitterness" signifies Simon was "in a state of nature and unregeneracy, under the power and dominion of covetousness, ambition, and hypocrisy"; the bitter root of sin in him, not the truth of grace. Barnes notes "one single sin may as certainly show that there is no true piety as many acts of iniquity"; Simon's offer revealing his state. The witnesses note Peter perceiving Simon in the gall of bitterness and bond of iniquity — under the dominion of sin, unregenerate. The witnesses note Simon's unregenerate state. The witnesses note the bond of iniquity.

The witnesses note Peter discerning Simon in the gall of bitterness — unregenerate, under the dominion of sin.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's discernment of Simon's unregenerate state. The lesson the expositors draw is the reality of Simon's bondage to sin, revealed by his act, and the discernment of the apostle. The witnesses note the discernment: "I perceive that thou art in the gall of bitterness, and in the bond of iniquity" — Peter, by the Spirit, perceives Simon's true state: not a converted man, but one in the gall of bitterness and the bond of iniquity. The expositors expound Gill's reading of the phrases: "in the gall of bitterness" signifies Simon was in a state of nature and unregeneracy, under the power of covetousness, ambition, and hypocrisy; the bitter root of sin in him (cf. Deuteronomy 29:18, "a root that beareth gall and wormwood"; Hebrews 12:15), not the grace of God; "in the bond of iniquity" — bound and held captive by sin, enslaved to his corruptions; the chains of iniquity upon him. The witnesses press the meaning: Simon was unregenerate — his heart not right (v.21), under the dominion of sin, captive to his covetous ambition; his profession and baptism notwithstanding, he was in bondage to iniquity, a slave to the sin that ruled him. The expositors draw the lesson Barnes marks: one single sin may as certainly show the absence of true piety as many acts; Simon's one offer revealed his whole state — covetous, ambitious, unregenerate; the act exposing the heart, the single sin the index of the soul. The witnesses observe the bondage of the unregenerate to sin: "the bond of iniquity" — the natural man is bound by sin, held captive, unable to free himself; Simon in this bondage, ruled by his corruptions; the state of all the unregenerate, slaves to sin until grace frees them (John 8:34, "whosoever committeth sin is the servant of sin"). The expositors note the discernment given the apostle: Peter perceived Simon's state — by the Spirit's gift of discerning, and by the evidence of his act; the apostle reading the heart through the deed; a warning that the discerning eye (and God) sees the true state beneath the profession. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the reality of Simon's bondage to sin, the single sin that revealed his state, and the bondage of the unregenerate from which only grace can free. The verse thus records Peter's discernment — Simon in the gall of bitterness and bond of iniquity — teaching the reality of his unregenerate state, revealed by his act, and the bondage of sin from which grace alone delivers.
24:
"Then answered Simon, and said, Pray ye to the Lord for me, that none of these things which ye have spoken come upon me." The witnesses note Simon's response and its doubtful nature. Barnes remarks: "Simon was directed to pray for himself (v.22), but he had no disposition to do that"; sinners "will often ask others to pray for them, when they are too proud, or too much in love with sin, to pray for themselves." He notes Simon's concern was that the threatened judgments not come upon him — fear of consequences, not true repentance. The witnesses note Simon asking the apostles to pray for him — seeking to escape the judgment, not to be cleansed of the sin; a response betraying fear, not repentance. The witnesses note Simon's response. The witnesses note the doubtful repentance.

The witnesses note Simon's response — asking others to pray, fearing the judgment rather than repenting of the sin.

So the witnesses make the verse Simon's response and its doubtful nature. The lesson the expositors draw is the difference between the fear of judgment and true repentance, betrayed in Simon's reply. The witnesses note the response: "Pray ye to the Lord for me, that none of these things which ye have spoken come upon me" — Simon asks the apostles to pray for him, that the threatened judgments not fall on him. The expositors observe what this reveals, which Barnes marks: Simon was directed to pray for himself (v.22), but had no disposition to do so; sinners often ask others to pray for them when they are too proud or too in love with sin to pray for themselves; Simon's asking others to pray, while not praying himself, a token of an unhumbled heart. The witnesses press the object of Simon's concern: "that none of these things... come upon me" — his concern was to escape the judgment, the threatened ruin, not to be cleansed of the sin; fear of the consequences, not sorrow for the offense; the response of one who dreads punishment, not of one who hates his sin.
