The expositors draw the lesson on the difference between fear and repentance: true repentance sorrows for sin and turns from it; Simon only feared the judgment and wished to escape it; the fear of consequences is not repentance, and may exist where there is no true contrition; Simon's reply the mark of an unrepentant heart dreading punishment. The witnesses observe the doubtful, even negative, witness of his words: the witnesses generally read Simon's response as showing no true repentance — his concern only for himself, his fear of judgment, his asking others to pray while not praying himself; the false convert unchanged, still in the gall of bitterness; tradition (and his later history as a heresiarch) confirming he did not truly repent. The expositors note the warning in Simon's case: a man may be alarmed, may fear judgment, may ask for prayer, and yet not truly repent; the difference between a frightened conscience and a broken heart; Simon the example of the alarmed but unrepentant sinner. The witnesses draw the lesson on true versus false response to conviction: the truly convicted repent and pray for themselves, sorrowing for sin; the merely frightened seek to escape the judgment, unchanged in heart; Simon the latter. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the difference between the fear of judgment and true repentance, the doubtful nature of Simon's response, and the warning against a frightened but unrepentant heart. The verse thus records Simon's response — pray ye for me, that the judgments come not — teaching the difference between the fear of judgment and true repentance, betrayed in his unhumbled reply.
25:
"And they, when they had testified and preached the word of the Lord, returned to Jerusalem, and preached the gospel in many villages of the Samaritans." The witnesses note the apostles' testimony and their preaching on the way back. Barnes notes that on their return they "travelled more at leisure, and preached in the villages also — a good example for the ministers of the gospel"; and that they returned to Jerusalem to bear witness to the resurrection in the city where Christ was crucified. The witnesses note Peter and John, having confirmed the Samaritan church, returning to Jerusalem and preaching the gospel in many Samaritan villages along the way — extending the mission. The witnesses note the apostles' return. The witnesses note the wider preaching.

The witnesses note the apostles confirming the church and preaching through the Samaritan villages on their return.

So the witnesses make the verse the apostles' testimony and wider preaching. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostles' confirming of the church and their diligence in preaching the gospel widely, even on their return. The witnesses note the testimony: "when they had testified and preached the word of the Lord" — Peter and John, having confirmed the Samaritan believers and conferred the gift, bore further testimony and preached the word; establishing the new church, confirming the gospel Philip had preached, and guarding against the errors of Simon. The expositors observe the return: "returned to Jerusalem" — the apostles went back to the mother church; Barnes notes they returned to bear witness to the resurrection of Christ in the very city where he was crucified, making Jerusalem their base. The witnesses press the wider preaching: "and preached the gospel in many villages of the Samaritans" — on their way back, the apostles did not travel in silence, but preached the gospel in many Samaritan villages; extending the mission beyond the city, sowing the word through the region. The expositors draw the lesson Barnes marks: a good example for ministers — the apostles, traveling, preached along the way, in the villages as well as the city; not wasting the journey, but using every opportunity to spread the gospel; diligence in preaching wherever they went. The witnesses observe the extension of the Samaritan mission: the gospel, having come to the city of Samaria, now spread through many villages; the apostles carrying it through the region; the mission to Samaria broadening, the word sown widely. The expositors note the contrast with Jewish prejudice: the apostles, Jews, preached freely in Samaritan villages — the old enmity overcome, the gospel embracing the Samaritans; Peter and John, who had once wished to call down fire on a Samaritan village (Luke 9:54), now preaching the gospel to many; the transforming power of the gospel over the old hatred. The witnesses draw the lesson on diligence in the gospel: the ministers of Christ are to use every opportunity, every journey, to spread the word; the apostles' example a pattern of diligent, opportunistic evangelism. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the apostles' confirming of the church, their diligence in preaching widely, and the gospel overcoming the old enmity. The verse thus records the apostles' testimony and wider preaching — confirming the church, preaching through the villages — teaching the confirming of the church and diligence in spreading the gospel widely.
26:
"And the angel of the Lord spake unto Philip, saying, Arise, and go toward the south unto the way that goeth down from Jerusalem unto Gaza, which is desert." The witnesses note the angelic direction sending Philip to a desert road. Chrysostom notes the providence in the route — "from Samaria it was towards the south... the same is desert: so that there is no fear of an attack from the Jews"; and marks Philip's prompt obedience: "he did not ask, Wherefore? but arose and went." The witnesses note the angel directing Philip from the success in Samaria to a desert road, without explanation — and Philip's ready obedience. The witnesses note the angelic direction. The witnesses note the desert road.

The witnesses note the angel sending Philip to a desert road — and Philip's prompt obedience without question.

So the witnesses make the verse the angelic direction of Philip. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that directed Philip and the prompt obedience that asked no question. The witnesses note the direction: "the angel of the Lord spake unto Philip, saying, Arise, and go toward the south unto the way that goeth down from Jerusalem unto Gaza, which is desert" — an angel directs Philip from Samaria southward, to a desert road; a specific, particular direction, sending him to a seemingly unpromising place. The expositors observe the strangeness of the call: Philip was sent from the great success in Samaria (a city won to Christ) to a desert road, where there seemed little prospect of fruit; the direction without explanation, the reason not given; God's ways often leading from evident success to seeming obscurity, the servant not knowing the purpose. The witnesses note Chrysostom's mark on the providence in the route: the way was desert, so there was no fear of attack from the Jews; the place chosen for the meeting to come (the eunuch) and safe for Philip; the providence ordering the route for the appointed encounter. The expositors press Philip's prompt obedience, which Chrysostom marks: "he did not ask, Wherefore? but arose and went" (v.27); Philip obeyed at once, without questioning the strange direction, without demanding the reason; the obedience of faith, ready to follow God's leading even when the purpose is hidden. The witnesses draw the lesson on obedience without explanation: God often directs his servants without telling them the reason; the duty is to obey, trusting that God has a purpose, though it be hidden; Philip's ready obedience to an unexplained call a pattern of faith. The expositors observe the providence behind the direction: God was sending Philip to meet the Ethiopian eunuch (v.27), a soul prepared and seeking; the desert road the appointed place of a great conversion; the seemingly unpromising direction leading to a glorious work; God's leading, however strange, always purposeful. The witnesses note the willingness of Philip to leave a fruitful field at God's command: he left the harvest in Samaria to go to a desert road, because God called; the servant's readiness to go where God sends, even from success to obscurity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providence that directs God's servants, the duty of obedience without demanding the reason, and the readiness to follow God's leading even from success to seeming obscurity. The verse thus records the angelic direction of Philip — to the desert road toward Gaza — teaching the providence that directed him and the prompt obedience that asked no question.
27:
"And he arose and went: and, behold, a man of Ethiopia, an eunuch of great authority under Candace queen of the Ethiopians, who had the charge of all her treasure, and had come to Jerusalem for to worship," The witnesses note the Ethiopian eunuch and his devotion. Chrysostom gives "high encomiums for the man": that he, "residing in Ethiopia and beset with so much business, and when there was no festival going on, and living in that superstitious city, came to Jerusalem for to worship"; great also his diligence in reading. Lightfoot discusses Candace and the eunuch's status. The witnesses note the eunuch — a man of great authority, treasurer to the queen, yet a devout worshipper who had come to Jerusalem to worship. The witnesses note the Ethiopian eunuch. The witnesses note his devotion.

The witnesses note the Ethiopian eunuch — a great official, yet a devout seeker who came far to worship at Jerusalem.

So the witnesses make the verse the introduction of the Ethiopian eunuch. The lesson the expositors draw is the devotion of this great official, a seeking soul whom God was about to meet. The witnesses note his rank: "a man of Ethiopia, an eunuch of great authority under Candace queen of the Ethiopians, who had the charge of all her treasure" — a man of high position, a court official, treasurer to the queen (Candace, a title of the Ethiopian queens, Lightfoot notes); a man of authority, wealth, and responsibility. The expositors observe his devotion, which Chrysostom praises with "high encomiums": that this man, residing in distant Ethiopia, burdened with great business, and living in a superstitious land, yet "came to Jerusalem for to worship" — and not even at a festival time; his devotion remarkable, seeking the true God at the temple, traveling far, amid his cares and his pagan surroundings; a devout seeker. The witnesses press the wonder of his seeking: a great man, occupied with affairs of state, from a far country, came to Jerusalem to worship the God of Israel — a proselyte or God-fearer, drawn to the truth; his rank and business no hindrance to his devotion; the seeking soul amid the press of the world. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence preparing the soul: God had prepared this man — drawing him to worship, to seek, to read the Scriptures (v.28); the seeking soul met by the sent evangelist; the providence working on both sides, preparing the eunuch and sending Philip. The witnesses observe Chrysostom's note on his diligence: great also his studiousness, reading the Scriptures even in his chariot on the journey (v.28); the devout man seeking knowledge of God, diligent in the word; his seeking rewarded with the gospel. The expositors note the breadth of the gospel's reach signaled here: an Ethiopian, from the ends of the then-known earth (cf. "Ethiopia shall soon stretch out her hands unto God," Psalm 68:31), brought to Christ; the gospel reaching beyond Judea and Samaria toward the nations; the eunuch a firstfruits of Africa. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the devotion of the great official, the seeking soul God prepared, and the gospel reaching toward the nations. The verse thus records the introduction of the Ethiopian eunuch — a great official, a devout worshipper — teaching the devotion of the seeking soul whom God was about to meet with the gospel.
28:
"Was returning, and sitting in his chariot read Esaias the prophet." The witnesses note the eunuch reading Isaiah on his journey home. Gill notes it remarkable that, though he must have heard of Jesus and the apostles in Jerusalem, he was not converted there; "his conversion being ordered to be at another time, in another place, and by another instrument." Barnes notes he read "doubtless the translation of Isaiah called" the Septuagint. The witnesses note the eunuch, returning from worship, diligently reading the prophet Isaiah in his chariot — the seeking soul searching the Scriptures, providentially at the very passage that pointed to Christ. The witnesses note the reading of Isaiah. The witnesses note the diligent seeker.

The witnesses note the eunuch reading Isaiah on his journey — the seeking soul searching the Scriptures, providentially prepared.

So the witnesses make the verse the eunuch reading Isaiah. The lesson the expositors draw is the diligence of the seeking soul in the Scriptures and the providence that prepared the meeting. The witnesses note the scene: "was returning, and sitting in his chariot read Esaias the prophet" — the eunuch, returning home from worship, spent his journey reading the prophet Isaiah; the devout man, not content with the worship at Jerusalem, continued seeking God in the Scriptures on the way home. The expositors observe the diligence this shows: the eunuch redeemed his travel-time for the word; reading the Scriptures in his chariot; the seeking soul diligent in the search, using every opportunity to know God; a pattern of devotion to the word. The witnesses press the providence in his reading: he was reading Isaiah — and (the next verses show) at the very passage (Isaiah 53) that pointed to the suffering Christ; the providence ordering not only the meeting but the very text, that the eunuch should be at the passage about Christ when Philip came; God preparing the soul and the Scripture together. The expositors note Gill's remarkable observation: though the eunuch had been in Jerusalem, where Jesus had been crucified and the apostles preached, he had not been converted there; his conversion ordered for another time, place, and instrument; the sovereignty of God in the timing and means of conversion, the eunuch met not in the city but on the desert road, by Philip. The witnesses observe the eunuch's searching despite not understanding: he read the Scripture though he did not yet understand it (v.31); the seeking soul searching even what it cannot grasp, diligent in the word, waiting for light; the honest seeker rewarded with a guide. The expositors note Barnes's point that he read the Septuagint (the Greek translation); the Scriptures accessible to him in a tongue he could read; the providence of the Scriptures translated, that the seeking soul might read. The witnesses draw the lesson on searching the Scriptures: the eunuch's diligent reading the pattern for the seeking soul; God meets those who search his word; the reading of Scripture the way the seeker is prepared for the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the diligence of the seeking soul, the providence that prepared the meeting and the text, and the searching of the Scriptures that prepares for the gospel. The verse thus records the eunuch reading Isaiah — searching the Scriptures on his journey — teaching the diligence of the seeking soul and the providence that prepared the meeting with the gospel.
29:
"Then the Spirit said unto Philip, Go near, and join thyself to this chariot." The witnesses note the Spirit's direction to Philip. Gill notes it was "the Holy Spirit... who spoke unto him, either by an articulate voice... or inwardly, by a secret impulse." Barnes observes "the thought in Philip's mind is here traced to his suggestion; all good thoughts and designs have the same origin"; and explains "join thyself" as "go and sit with him." The witnesses note the Holy Spirit directing Philip to draw near and join the eunuch's chariot — the divine guidance of the gospel's advance to the seeking soul. The witnesses note the Spirit's direction. The witnesses note the divine guidance.

The witnesses note the Spirit directing Philip to the chariot — the divine guidance bringing the evangelist to the seeking soul.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit's direction to Philip. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine guidance that brought the evangelist to the seeking soul, and the Spirit as the source of good thoughts and designs. The witnesses note the direction: "the Spirit said unto Philip, Go near, and join thyself to this chariot" — the Holy Spirit directs Philip to draw near and join the eunuch's chariot; Gill notes the Spirit spoke either by an articulate voice or by a secret inward impulse; the divine guidance moving Philip to the meeting. The expositors observe the source of the impulse Barnes marks: the thought in Philip's mind to approach the eunuch is here traced to the Spirit's suggestion; all good thoughts and designs have the same origin — the Spirit of God; the holy impulses that move God's servants to good are from the Spirit. The witnesses press the divine guidance of the whole encounter: the angel sent Philip to the road (v.26), the Spirit now directs him to the chariot; the meeting wholly ordered by God — the seeking soul prepared, the evangelist sent and directed; the divine guidance bringing the two together for the conversion. The expositors draw the lesson on the Spirit's leading: God's servants are led by the Spirit to their work, to the souls they are to reach; the Spirit directing the evangelist to the prepared soul; the guidance of the Spirit essential to fruitful ministry. The witnesses observe the simplicity of the direction: "go near, and join thyself to this chariot" — a plain, specific command; Philip to draw near, to join the eunuch, to enter into conversation; the Spirit directing the practical step that would lead to the gospel. The expositors note the providence converging: the eunuch reading Isaiah, the Spirit directing Philip; the prepared soul and the directed evangelist brought together at the appointed moment; the providence of God ordering every detail for the conversion. The witnesses draw the lesson on following the Spirit's leading: Philip would obey at once (v.30); the believer is to follow the Spirit's impulses to good, to draw near to the souls God brings in his way, to seize the opportunity; the Spirit's leading to be heeded. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the divine guidance bringing the evangelist to the seeking soul, the Spirit as the source of good impulses, and the duty of following the Spirit's leading. The verse thus records the Spirit's direction to Philip — go near and join the chariot — teaching the divine guidance that brought the evangelist to the seeking soul and the Spirit as the source of good thoughts and designs.
30:
"And Philip ran thither to him, and heard him read the prophet Esaias, and said, Understandest thou what thou readest?" The witnesses note Philip's eager obedience and his wise opening question. Barnes marks the haste: "Philip ran... indicating his haste, and his desire to obey the suggestions of the Spirit"; though many difficulties might have been raised (the eunuch a stranger, of rank, engaged in reading), "Philip obeyed without any hesitation... it is well to follow the first suggestions of the Spirit." The witnesses note Philip running to the chariot, hearing the eunuch read Isaiah, and asking whether he understood — the eager, wise approach that opened the way for the gospel. The witnesses note Philip's eager obedience. The witnesses note the opening question.

The witnesses note Philip running to obey and his wise question — opening the way for the gospel to the seeking soul.

So the witnesses make the verse Philip's eager obedience and wise question. The lesson the expositors draw is the prompt obedience to the Spirit and the wise approach that opened the way for the gospel. The witnesses note the haste: "Philip ran thither to him" — Barnes marks the running as indicating Philip's haste and his eagerness to obey the Spirit's suggestion; not slow or reluctant, but running to do the Spirit's bidding; the promptness of glad obedience. The expositors observe the difficulties Philip might have raised, which Barnes lists: the eunuch was a stranger, a man of rank, engaged in reading, perhaps unwilling to be interrupted; a hesitant man might have found reasons to hold back; but Philip obeyed without hesitation, running to the chariot; the lesson — to follow the first suggestions of the Spirit, to obey at once, not to reason away the call. The witnesses note what Philip heard: "heard him read the prophet Esaias" — drawing near, Philip heard the eunuch reading Isaiah aloud (as was the custom); the providence giving Philip his opening, the very Scripture the eunuch read. The expositors press the wisdom of the opening question: "Understandest thou what thou readest?" — a wise, gentle, fitting question; not abrupt or presumptuous, but inviting; meeting the eunuch where he was (in the Scripture), drawing out his need, opening the way for instruction; the question that engaged the seeker and led to the gospel. The witnesses draw the lesson on the wise approach to souls: Philip began where the eunuch was, with the Scripture he read, and with a question that drew out his need; the skillful, tactful approach of the soul-winner, meeting men where they are, engaging their attention, leading them to the truth; a pattern of personal evangelism. The expositors observe the eunuch's honest readiness (v.31): the question met a humble, seeking heart, ready to confess its need; the wise approach finding a prepared soul; the question and the readiness meeting. The witnesses note the convergence of the Spirit's leading and Philip's wisdom: the Spirit directed Philip to the chariot, and Philip wisely opened the conversation; the divine guidance and the human skill together; the Spirit using the wise approach of his servant. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Philip's prompt obedience, the wisdom of beginning where the soul is, and the gentle question that opens the way for the gospel. The verse thus records Philip's eager obedience and wise question — running to the chariot, asking if he understood — teaching prompt obedience to the Spirit and the wise approach that opened the way for the gospel.
31:
"And he said, How can I, except some man should guide me? And he desired Philip that he would come up and sit with him." The witnesses note the eunuch's humility and his desire for instruction. Gill commends his spirit: "instead of answering in a haughty and disdainful manner, as great men are too apt to do," he "expresses himself with great and uncommon modesty; with a sense and confession of his ignorance... and of the necessity and usefulness of the instructions of men, appointed of God to open and explain the Scriptures." The witnesses note the great man humbly confessing his need of a guide, and inviting Philip to sit with him and teach — the humility of the true seeker. The witnesses note the eunuch's humility. The witnesses note the desire for guidance.

The witnesses note the eunuch's humble confession of need — inviting Philip to guide him in the Scripture.

So the witnesses make the verse the eunuch's humility and desire for instruction. The lesson the expositors draw is the humility of the true seeker, confessing his need of a guide and welcoming instruction. The witnesses note the humble confession: "How can I, except some man should guide me?" — the eunuch, a great man, confesses his ignorance and his need of a guide to understand the Scripture; Gill commends his uncommon modesty — not answering haughtily, as great men are apt to do, but confessing his ignorance and the usefulness of the instruction of those appointed by God to open the Scriptures. The expositors observe the humility this displays: a man of rank and authority, humbly owning that he cannot understand the Scripture without a guide; the pride of place laid aside, the teachable spirit of the seeker; the great man willing to be taught, even by a stranger. The witnesses press the lesson on the need of a guide: the eunuch's confession teaches that the Scriptures need to be opened and explained; that God has appointed teachers to guide men into their meaning; that even the diligent reader needs instruction; the means of the ministry necessary for understanding the word (cf. the apostles' opening of the Scriptures, Luke 24:45). The expositors draw the lesson on the teachable spirit: the true seeker is humble, owning his ignorance, willing to be taught; the proud, who think they need no guide, remain in darkness; the humble, who confess their need, are taught; "the meek will he guide in judgment" (Psalm 25:9). The witnesses note the invitation: "he desired Philip that he would come up and sit with him" — the eunuch invited Philip into his chariot to teach him; welcoming the instruction, honoring the teacher, making room for the word; the seeker's eagerness to learn. The expositors observe the providence rewarded: the eunuch's humble seeking and reading were rewarded with a guide sent by God; the seeking soul, confessing its need, met with the very instruction it required; God providing the teacher for the teachable. The witnesses draw the lesson on receiving instruction: the believer, like the eunuch, is to own his need of a guide, to welcome the opening of the Scriptures, to be teachable; the humility that confesses ignorance the way to understanding. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the humility of the true seeker, the need of a guide to the Scriptures, and the teachable spirit that welcomes instruction. The verse thus records the eunuch's humble confession and invitation — how can I, except some guide me? — teaching the humility of the true seeker and the need of instruction to understand the word.
32:
"The place of the scripture which he read was this, He was led as a sheep to the slaughter; and like a lamb dumb before his shearer, so opened he not his mouth:" The witnesses note the passage of Isaiah the eunuch read — the suffering Servant. Gill notes the Scripture was already divided into sections, and that the text is given according to the Greek version (Septuagint); and discusses whether the eunuch used that version. Lightfoot raises the questions of the eunuch's status (Jew, proselyte, or Gentile) and the version used. The witnesses note the eunuch reading Isaiah 53 — the prophecy of the Lamb led silently to the slaughter, the suffering Christ. The witnesses note the suffering Servant passage. The witnesses note the silent Lamb.

The witnesses note the eunuch reading Isaiah 53 — the Lamb led silently to the slaughter, the suffering Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the passage of Isaiah the eunuch read. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that set the eunuch at the great prophecy of the suffering Christ, the silent Lamb. The witnesses note the text: "He was led as a sheep to the slaughter; and like a lamb dumb before his shearer, so opened he not his mouth" — the eunuch was reading Isaiah 53:7, the prophecy of the suffering Servant, led silently to his death like a sheep to slaughter, like a lamb dumb before its shearer. The expositors observe the providence in the very passage: of all the Scriptures, the eunuch was reading the chapter that most clearly foretold the suffering and death of Christ — Isaiah 53, the "gospel in the Old Testament"; the providence ordering that the seeker should be at this passage when Philip came; God preparing the very text for the gospel. The witnesses press the meaning of the prophecy: the suffering Servant led "as a sheep to the slaughter," silent and unresisting "like a lamb dumb before his shearer"; the prophecy of Christ's patient, silent suffering — led to death without resistance, opening not his mouth, enduring in silence (fulfilled in his trials, Matthew 27:12-14); the Lamb of God going meekly to the slaughter. The expositors draw the lesson on the silent Lamb: Christ, the true Lamb, was led to the slaughter and opened not his mouth — silent under accusation, patient under suffering, meek before his shearers; the pattern of patient endurance and the proof of his willing sacrifice; "as a lamb to the slaughter" (Isaiah 53:7), the meekness of the suffering Saviour. The witnesses note Gill's and Lightfoot's observations on the text: the Scripture already divided into sections; the text given according to the Septuagint; the questions of the eunuch's status (Jew, proselyte, or Gentile) and the version he used — points of detail, the substance being the great prophecy of the suffering Christ. The expositors observe the fitness of this passage for the eunuch's conversion: reading of the suffering Servant, he would ask of whom the prophet spoke (v.34), and Philip would preach Jesus; the passage the door to the gospel; the suffering Christ the very heart of the message. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providence that set the eunuch at Isaiah 53, the prophecy of the silent Lamb, and the suffering Christ as the heart of the gospel. The verse thus records the passage the eunuch read — the Lamb led silently to the slaughter — teaching the providence that prepared the great prophecy of the suffering Christ for the seeker's conversion.
33:
"In his humiliation his judgment was taken away: and who shall declare his generation? for his life is taken from the earth." The witnesses note the continuation of the prophecy of Christ's humiliation. Gill expounds "his humiliation" as Christ's low estate — his assumption of human nature, his mean parentage, his sorrows, his being apprehended, scourged, condemned, mocked, and crucified; in this low estate "his judgment was taken away" — justice denied him. The witnesses note the prophecy describing Christ's humiliation, the denial of justice in his condemnation, and his being cut off from the earth — the suffering Servant brought low. The witnesses note Christ's humiliation. The witnesses note justice denied.

The witnesses note the prophecy of Christ's humiliation — justice denied, his life taken from the earth.

So the witnesses make the verse the prophecy of Christ's humiliation. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of Christ's humiliation foretold — justice denied, his life cut off. The witnesses note the continuation of Isaiah's prophecy: "In his humiliation his judgment was taken away: and who shall declare his generation? for his life is taken from the earth." The expositors expound Gill's reading of "his humiliation": Christ's low estate — his taking of human nature with its weaknesses, his mean parentage and education, his sorrows from the cradle to the cross, his agony, his being apprehended, bound, scourged, condemned by both Sanhedrin and governor, mocked, and crucified; the depth of his humiliation, the Servant brought low. The witnesses press the meaning of "his judgment was taken away": in his humiliation, justice was denied him — he was condemned without just cause, denied a fair trial, his rights taken away; the innocent treated as guilty, justice perverted in his condemnation; the suffering Servant denied the justice due him. The expositors weigh the difficult phrase "who shall declare his generation?": variously understood — of the wickedness of the generation that condemned him (none can declare or describe their wickedness); or of the endless duration of his life (who can declare his eternal generation as the Son, or his unending life after the resurrection); or of his spiritual offspring (who can number his generation, the multitude of his seed); the witnesses note the range of readings, the phrase admitting several senses. The witnesses note "his life is taken from the earth": Christ was cut off, his life taken from the earth in death; the suffering Servant brought to death, his earthly life ended; the prophecy of his being cut off (Daniel 9:26). The expositors draw the lesson on the humiliation of Christ: the depth to which the Son of God descended — humbled, denied justice, cut off; the prophecy foretelling the full abasement of the suffering Servant; the wonder of his condescension for our salvation. The witnesses observe the fitness for the eunuch: reading of this humiliation, the eunuch would ask of whom the prophet spoke (v.34); the prophecy of the suffering, humbled Christ the door to the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the depth of Christ's humiliation foretold, the justice denied him, and the suffering Servant cut off for us. The verse thus records the prophecy of Christ's humiliation — justice denied, his life taken — teaching the depth of his abasement as the suffering Servant.
34:
"And the eunuch answered Philip, and said, I pray thee, of whom speaketh the prophet this? of himself, or of some other man?" The witnesses note the eunuch's earnest inquiry. Barnes notes "this was a natural inquiry, for there was nothing in the text itself that would determine expressly to whom the reference was"; and that "the ancient Jews expressly applied the passage to the Messiah" (citing the Targum), though the eunuch may not have known this. The witnesses note the eunuch earnestly asking of whom the prophet spoke — the very question that opened the way for Philip to preach Christ. The witnesses note the eunuch's question. The witnesses note the open door.

The witnesses note the eunuch's earnest question — of whom speaks the prophet? — opening the way to preach Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the eunuch's earnest inquiry. The lesson the expositors draw is the honest, seeking question that opened the way for the gospel. The witnesses note the question: "of whom speaketh the prophet this? of himself, or of some other man?" — the eunuch, perplexed by the passage, earnestly asks Philip of whom the prophet spoke; whether of himself (Isaiah) or of another. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the naturalness of the inquiry: there was nothing in the text itself to determine expressly to whom it referred; the eunuch's question was a natural, honest perplexity, seeking to know the subject of the prophecy; the genuine inquiry of a seeking, puzzled soul. The witnesses press the openness of the eunuch's heart in the question: he did not dismiss the difficulty, nor presume to decide, but humbly asked, seeking light; the teachable spirit (v.31) here seeking the meaning; the honest question of one ready to learn. The expositors note Barnes's observation that the ancient Jews applied this passage to the Messiah (the Targum rendering Isaiah 52:13, "Behold my servant, the Messiah, shall be prospered"); though the eunuch, perhaps not knowing this, asked sincerely; the passage rightly understood of the Messiah, the eunuch seeking its true subject. The witnesses draw the lesson on the providence in the question: the eunuch's inquiry was the very door Philip needed — asking of whom the prophet spoke, he opened the way for Philip to preach Jesus (v.35); the honest question the occasion of the gospel; God ordering the seeker's perplexity to lead to the truth. The expositors observe the value of the honest inquiry: the eunuch's question, springing from a sincere desire to understand, was met with the gospel; the seeking soul that asks honestly is answered; "ask, and it shall be given you" (Matthew 7:7); the honest question rewarded with the truth. The witnesses note the contrast with the proud who dismiss what they cannot understand: the eunuch did not reject the passage as obscure, nor presume to interpret it himself, but asked; the humility that seeks understanding the way to the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the honest, seeking question, the open door it gave for the gospel, and the value of humble inquiry that seeks the truth. The verse thus records the eunuch's earnest question — of whom speaks the prophet? — teaching the honest inquiry that opened the way for Philip to preach Christ.
35:
"Then Philip opened his mouth, and began at the same scripture, and preached unto him Jesus." The witnesses note Philip preaching Christ from Isaiah. Gill notes "opened his mouth" is a phrase for solemn, free, and clear teaching, frequent in Jewish writings for expounding Scripture. Barnes explains Philip, "taking this as a text to be illustrated," "showed him that Jesus of Nazareth exactly answered to the description of the prophet." The witnesses note Philip, beginning at the very passage of Isaiah 53 the eunuch read, preaching Jesus to him — the suffering Servant identified as the crucified Christ. The witnesses note Philip preaching Jesus. The witnesses note Christ from Isaiah.

The witnesses note Philip preaching Jesus from Isaiah 53 — the suffering Servant identified as the crucified Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse Philip preaching Christ from Isaiah. The lesson the expositors draw is that Philip preached Jesus from the very Scripture the eunuch read, identifying the suffering Servant as the crucified Christ. The witnesses note the solemn teaching: "Philip opened his mouth" — Gill notes this phrase, frequent in Jewish writings, denotes solemn, free, and clear teaching, expounding the Scripture with authority and openness; Philip beginning his exposition. The expositors observe the text he began from: "began at the same scripture" — Philip took the very passage the eunuch was reading (Isaiah 53) as his text; meeting the seeker where he was, in the Scripture before him; the wise teacher beginning from the text in hand. The witnesses press the substance of his preaching: "and preached unto him Jesus" — Philip preached Jesus; Barnes explains he showed the eunuch that Jesus of Nazareth exactly answered to the prophet's description; the suffering Servant of Isaiah 53 identified as the crucified Christ; the prophecy fulfilled in Jesus. The expositors draw the lesson on preaching Christ from the Scriptures: Philip preached Jesus from Isaiah — showing that the suffering Servant, led as a lamb to the slaughter, was Jesus; the Old Testament prophecy opened to reveal Christ; the right preaching of Scripture leading to Jesus; "in the volume of the book it is written of me" (Psalm 40:7). The witnesses observe the fitness of preaching Jesus from this passage: Isaiah 53, the prophecy of the suffering, dying Servant, is the very heart of the gospel — Christ's atoning death foretold; Philip, beginning here, preached the whole gospel of the crucified Saviour; the suffering Servant the door to the cross and the resurrection. The expositors note the wisdom of Philip's method: he began at the text the eunuch read, met his question, and preached Jesus; the personal, scriptural, Christ-centered approach of the evangelist; preaching not himself, not abstractions, but Jesus, from the Scripture. The witnesses draw the lesson on the substance of all true preaching: to preach Jesus — his person, his suffering, his death, his resurrection; the suffering Servant of Isaiah the crucified and risen Christ; the gospel the preaching of Jesus from the Scriptures. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Philip's preaching of Jesus from Isaiah, the suffering Servant identified as Christ, and Jesus as the substance of all true preaching. The verse thus records Philip preaching Christ from Isaiah — beginning at the same Scripture, preaching Jesus — teaching that the suffering Servant is the crucified Christ, the substance of the gospel.
36:
"And as they went on their way, they came unto a certain water: and the eunuch said, See, here is water; what doth hinder me to be baptized?" The witnesses note the eunuch's desire for baptism. Gill notes that as they traveled, Philip preaching and the eunuch hearing, they came to water, and the eunuch, having been instructed about Christ and baptism, requested it. Barnes notes the text does not determine whether the water was a river, brook, or pool. The witnesses note the eunuch, having heard the gospel and evidently been taught about baptism, eagerly asking to be baptized at the first water — the fruit of faith seeking the ordinance. The witnesses note the eunuch's request. The witnesses note the desire for baptism.

The witnesses note the eunuch eagerly asking for baptism — the fruit of faith seeking the ordinance.

So the witnesses make the verse the eunuch's desire for baptism. The lesson the expositors draw is the fruit of faith in the eunuch's eager desire for baptism upon believing. The witnesses note the occasion: "as they went on their way, they came unto a certain water" — as they traveled, Philip preaching and the eunuch hearing and believing, they came to water (Barnes notes the text does not specify whether a river, brook, or pool). The expositors observe the eunuch's request: "See, here is water; what doth hinder me to be baptized?" — the eunuch, having heard the gospel and evidently been instructed about baptism (for he knew to ask for it), eagerly sought to be baptized at the first water; the fruit of faith seeking the ordinance Christ appointed. The witnesses press the eagerness of the new believer: the eunuch, having believed in Christ, immediately desired baptism — the outward confession and sign of his faith; not delaying, but eager to obey Christ and to be received by the ordinance; the readiness of true faith to confess Christ. The expositors draw the lesson on baptism following faith: the eunuch, having believed (the next verse confesses his faith), sought baptism; the proper order — faith, then baptism; the ordinance the sign and seal of the faith already received; the new believer eager to obey. The witnesses observe that Philip must have taught the eunuch about baptism in preaching Jesus: for the eunuch knew to ask for it; preaching Christ includes teaching his ordinances; the gospel and its sign taught together. The expositors note the eunuch's eagerness as a model: the new convert, eager to confess Christ and obey him, sought baptism at once; the readiness to take up the duties and privileges of the faith; the fruit of a true and lively faith. The witnesses draw the lesson on confessing Christ: baptism the appointed confession of faith; the eunuch's eager request a model of the new believer's readiness to confess Christ openly; faith seeking its outward expression. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the eunuch's eager desire for baptism, the fruit of faith seeking the ordinance, and the readiness of true faith to confess Christ. The verse thus records the eunuch's request for baptism — what doth hinder me? — teaching the fruit of faith in the eager desire for baptism upon believing.
37:
"And Philip said, If thou believest with all thine heart, thou mayest. And he answered and said, I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God." The witnesses note the confession of faith required before baptism, and a textual question. Gill draws the requirement: "faith in Christ, and a profession of it, are necessary prerequisites to baptism... and this faith should not be a mere historical and temporary faith, nor a feigned one, but a believing in Christ with the heart unto righteousness." The witnesses note Philip requiring a heartfelt confession of faith before baptism, and the eunuch confessing Jesus as the Son of God — the necessity of faith confessed before baptism (the verse, though absent from many manuscripts, expressing a sound principle). The witnesses note the confession of faith. The witnesses note faith before baptism.

The witnesses note the confession of faith required before baptism — the eunuch owning Jesus as the Son of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the confession of faith before baptism. The lesson the expositors draw is the necessity of a heartfelt faith and its confession before baptism. The witnesses note Philip's condition: "If thou believest with all thine heart, thou mayest" — Philip requires a heartfelt faith before baptism; not a mere outward request, but a true, whole-hearted believing; the condition of the ordinance. The expositors press Gill's drawing-out of the requirement: faith in Christ, and a profession of it, are necessary prerequisites to baptism; and the faith required is not a mere historical, temporary, or feigned faith (such as Simon's, v.13), but a believing in Christ with the heart unto righteousness (Romans 10:10); the heartfelt, saving faith the condition of baptism. The witnesses note the eunuch's confession: "I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God" — the eunuch confesses his faith, owning Jesus Christ as the Son of God; the good confession (cf. 1 Timothy 6:12), the heart's belief expressed in the mouth's confession; the proper response, faith confessed before baptism. The expositors draw the lesson on faith and confession before baptism: baptism is for believers, who confess their faith; the order — faith, confession, then baptism; the eunuch's heartfelt confession the model of the baptismal confession; "with the heart man believeth... and with the mouth confession is made" (Romans 10:10). The witnesses note the textual question honestly: this verse (37) is absent from many of the best and most ancient manuscripts, and is omitted by many editors as a likely early addition; yet the principle it expresses — that faith and its confession should precede baptism — is sound and scriptural, drawn from the whole tenor of the New Testament; the verse's textual standing uncertain, but its doctrine true. The expositors observe the substance of the confession: "Jesus Christ is the Son of God" — the heart of the Christian confession, the deity and Messiahship of Jesus; the eunuch confessing the central truth; the good confession on which baptism rests. The witnesses draw the lesson on the necessity of personal faith: baptism without faith profits nothing; the eunuch's heartfelt confession the proper ground; faith in Christ confessed the condition of the ordinance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the necessity of heartfelt faith confessed before baptism, the good confession of the eunuch, and the honest noting of the textual question that leaves the principle sound. The verse thus records the confession of faith before baptism — if thou believest, the eunuch confessing the Son of God — teaching the necessity of heartfelt faith and its confession before baptism, amid a noted textual question.
38:
"And he commanded the chariot to stand still: and they went down both into the water, both Philip and the eunuch; and he baptized him." The witnesses note the baptism of the eunuch and the question of its mode. Gill (a Baptist) takes "they went down both into the water" as evidence that "baptism was then performed by immersion." Barnes cautions that the preposition "into" (eis) "does not of necessity mean that," but might be expressed by "to" or "unto" — "they went to the water," without determining whether they went into it. The witnesses note the eunuch and Philip going down to the water and the baptism administered — the mode (immersion or otherwise) discussed from the prepositions. The witnesses note the baptism. The witnesses note the mode debated.

The witnesses note the eunuch's baptism — the mode (immersion or not) weighed from the prepositions.

So the witnesses make the verse the baptism of the eunuch and the question of its mode. The lesson the expositors draw is the administration of baptism upon confession, and the differing readings of its mode. The witnesses note the act: "he commanded the chariot to stand still: and they went down both into the water, both Philip and the eunuch; and he baptized him" — the eunuch stopped the chariot, and both he and Philip went down to the water, and Philip baptized him; the ordinance administered upon the confession of faith. The expositors weigh the question of mode that the verse has occasioned. Gill (a Baptist) takes "they went down both into the water" and (v.39) "came up out of the water" as evidence that baptism was performed by immersion — the going down into and coming up out of the water indicating the dipping of the whole person. Barnes cautions, on the other side, that the preposition "into" (eis) does not necessarily mean entrance into the water, but might be rendered "to" or "unto" — "they went to the water" — without determining whether they entered it; the prepositions, he holds, not deciding the mode. The witnesses note the long and earnest discussion this verse has occasioned between those who hold immersion the proper or only mode and those who allow other modes (pouring, sprinkling); the immersionists pressing the "down into" and "up out of," the others noting the prepositions admit other senses; the debate of long standing, the verse cited on both sides. The expositors draw the lesson, holding the substance while noting the difference: the certain point is that the eunuch, upon his confession, was baptized — received by the ordinance Christ appointed; the mode (immersion, pouring, or sprinkling) the point of difference, the witnesses divided, the prepositions not decisive. The witnesses observe the agreement amid the difference: all agree that baptism was administered, that it followed faith and confession, and that it was the appointed sign of the eunuch's reception into the church; the mode debated, the ordinance and its meaning agreed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the baptism of the eunuch upon confession, the differing readings of its mode, and the agreement on the ordinance and its meaning. The verse thus records the baptism of the eunuch — going down to the water, baptized — teaching the administration of baptism upon confession and the long-debated question of its mode.
39:
"And when they were come up out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord caught away Philip, that the eunuch saw him no more: and he went on his way rejoicing." The witnesses note Philip's miraculous removal and the eunuch's joy. Gill again takes "come up out of the water" as showing baptism by immersion. Barnes notes the preposition "out of" (ek) stands opposed to "into," and that the Spirit who had directed Philip now caught him away. The witnesses note the Spirit miraculously catching Philip away, so the eunuch saw him no more, and the eunuch going on his way rejoicing — the new convert's joy in his salvation. The witnesses note Philip caught away. The witnesses note the eunuch's joy.

The witnesses note Philip miraculously caught away and the eunuch going on rejoicing — the new convert's joy.

So the witnesses make the verse Philip's removal and the eunuch's joy. The lesson the expositors draw is the miraculous removal of Philip and the joy of the new convert in his salvation. The witnesses note the miracle: "the Spirit of the Lord caught away Philip, that the eunuch saw him no more" — the Holy Spirit miraculously caught Philip away, removing him suddenly from the eunuch's sight; the Spirit who had directed Philip to the chariot (v.29) now bearing him away to his next work (v.40); a miraculous transport, marking the divine guidance of the whole encounter. The expositors observe the purpose of the sudden removal: Philip's work with the eunuch was done — the gospel preached, the man baptized; and the Spirit caught him away to other service; the abrupt removal perhaps also confirming to the eunuch the divine hand in the meeting, that his teacher was a man sent and led by God. The witnesses note Gill's continued argument on the mode: "come up out of the water" again taken (with v.38) as evidence of immersion; Barnes noting the preposition "out of" (ek) opposed to "into"; the mode-debate continuing, the prepositions weighed. The expositors press the eunuch's joy: "he went on his way rejoicing" — the eunuch, having believed and been baptized, went on his journey rejoicing; the joy of salvation, of sins forgiven, of Christ found; the new convert's gladness, though his teacher was gone. The witnesses draw the lesson on the joy of salvation: the eunuch rejoiced — not in his teacher (now gone), but in his Saviour; the joy of the gospel, of faith in Christ, of the new life; "joy in the Holy Ghost" (Romans 14:17); the new convert going on his way with gladness. The expositors observe that the eunuch did not grieve at Philip's removal, but rejoiced; his joy not in the man but in the Christ the man had preached; the convert resting in the Saviour, not the instrument; the joy abiding though the teacher departed. The witnesses note the providence completing the work: the eunuch, converted and baptized, went home to Ethiopia (tradition holds he carried the gospel there); the gospel reaching the ends of the earth through this one seeking soul; the firstfruits of Africa going on rejoicing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the miraculous removal of Philip, the joy of the new convert in his salvation, and the gladness that rests in Christ, not the instrument. The verse thus records Philip caught away and the eunuch rejoicing — the Spirit removing the evangelist, the convert going on with joy — teaching the divine guidance of the encounter and the joy of the new convert in his salvation.
40:
"But Philip was found at Azotus: and passing through he preached in all the cities, till he came to Caesarea." The witnesses note Philip's continued ministry after his removal. Lightfoot calculates the distance from Gaza to Azotus (about thirty-four miles), and traces the route through the coastal cities. Barnes notes Philip "came to Azotus" (the Ashdod of the Philistines), and preached through all the cities to Caesarea. The witnesses note Philip, caught away, found at Azotus, and thence preaching the gospel through all the coastal cities until he reached Caesarea — the evangelist's continued, diligent ministry. The witnesses note Philip's continued ministry. The witnesses note the preaching through the cities.

The witnesses note Philip preaching through the cities to Caesarea — the evangelist's continued, diligent ministry.

So the witnesses make the verse Philip's continued ministry. The lesson the expositors draw is the evangelist's diligent, continued preaching through the cities, the gospel spreading along his way. The witnesses note Philip's reappearance: "Philip was found at Azotus" — after being caught away (v.39), Philip was found at Azotus (Barnes notes, the Ashdod of the Philistines), some distance from Gaza (Lightfoot calculates about thirty-four miles); the Spirit having borne him there to continue his work. The expositors observe his continued preaching: "passing through he preached in all the cities, till he came to Caesarea" — Philip, journeying from Azotus, preached the gospel in all the cities along the coastal route, until he reached Caesarea; the evangelist diligently preaching through the region, sowing the word in city after city. The witnesses press the diligence of Philip: he did not rest after the great work with the eunuch, but went on preaching through all the cities; the tireless evangelist, using every place and opportunity; the gospel spreading along his path. The expositors note Lightfoot's tracing of the route through the coastal cities (Azotus, Ashkelon, Jamnia, and on to Caesarea); the geographical detail grounding the account in real places; the evangelist's circuit through the land. The witnesses observe the providence in Philip's ministry: from the desert road and the one eunuch, the Spirit bore Philip to a wide field, preaching through many cities; the gospel spreading from the single conversion to the whole region; the evangelist's work multiplied. The expositors note that Philip settled at length at Caesarea, where he is later found (Acts 21:8, "Philip the evangelist, which was one of the seven"); the evangelist's circuit ending at the city that became his home; the faithful servant continuing his work. The witnesses draw the lesson on diligent, continued ministry: Philip's tireless preaching through the cities the pattern of the faithful evangelist; using every place and opportunity, never resting from the work; the gospel spread by his diligence. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Philip's continued, diligent ministry, the gospel spreading through the cities, and the tireless faithfulness of the evangelist. The verse thus records Philip's continued ministry — found at Azotus, preaching through all the cities to Caesarea — teaching the diligent, continued preaching of the evangelist and the gospel spreading along his way.
